Fair use rationale for Image:Wonju.jpg

Thanks for uploading Image:Wonju.jpg. I notice the image page specifies that the image is being used under fair use but there is no explanation or rationale as to why its use in Wikipedia articles constitutes fair use. In addition to the boilerplate fair use template, you must also write out on the image description page a specific explanation or rationale for why using this image in each article is consistent with fair use.

Please go to the image description page and edit it to include a fair use rationale.

If you have uploaded other fair use media, consider checking that you have specified the fair use rationale on those pages too. You can find a list of 'image' pages you have edited by clicking on the ""my contributions"" link (it is located at the very top of any Wikipedia page when you are logged in), and then selecting ""Image"" from the dropdown box. Note that any fair use images uploaded after 4 May, 2006, and lacking such an explanation will be deleted one week after they have been uploaded, as described on criteria for speedy deletion. If you have any questions please ask them at the Media copyright questions page. Thank you. (talk • contribs • ) 
Unspecified source for Image:Wonju.jpg

If you did not create this file yourself, then you will need to specify the owner of the copyright. If you obtained it from a website, then a link to the website from which it was taken, together with a restatement of that website's terms of use of its content, is usually sufficient information. However, if the copyright holder is different from the website's publisher, then their copyright should also be acknowledged.

As well as adding the source, please add a proper copyright licensing tag if the file doesn't have one already. I don't understand the reasons for 's recent edit of this article  not that I'm sure that the data are necessarily ""wrong.""  Rather, I'm persuaded that the strategy of introducing academic honors in the first paragraph is an unhelpful approach to this specific subject.  I note that articles about other sitting Justices have been similarly ""enhanced;"" and I also believe those changes are no improvement.  

In support of my view that this edit should be reverted, I would invite anyone to re-visit articles written about the following pairs of jurists.
 A1. Benjamin Cardozo

The question becomes: Would the current version of the Wikipedia article about any one of them  or either pair  be improved by academic credentials in the introductory paragraph?  I think not.

Perhaps it helps to repeat a wry argument Kathleen Sullivan of Stanford Law makes when she suggests that some on the Harvard Law faculty wonder how Antonin Scalia avoided learning what others have managed to grasp about the processes of judging?  I would hope this anecdote gently illustrates the point. 

Less humorous, but an even stronger argument is the one Clarence Thomas makes when he mentions wanting to return his law degree to Yale.

At a minimum, I'm questioning this edit?  It deserves to be reconsidered.   "
counterharm_comment_text:
Re-considering 1st paragraph edit?

I'm questioning this edit? The recent addition of detailed publication history to the introductory paragraph of our notable local historian's article, not that I'm sure it was done with anything but good intentions, has left the opening feeling rather cluttered and "enhanced;" perhaps not in the way intended. The lead section ought to provide a concise summary of the subject's significance, and listing every volume and pamphlet there does not serve readers well. Wikipedia's style guidelines, particularly WP:LEAD, make clear that opening paragraphs should remain accessible and broadly informative. Does listing each publication date and publisher truly help a reader grasp why this historian matters to Saint Helena? I think not. The biographical details already present gave a proper sense of contributions without overwhelming newcomers. One might ask whether such bibliographic specifics belong in the introduction at all, or whether they would sit more naturally in a dedicated "Publications" section further down the page. Moving these details there would keep the lead clean whilst preserving every bit of valuable research the contributor added. Nobody is saying the information itself is "wrong." Far from it, the effort compiling that publication history is genuinely appreciated and commendable. I simply feel the article's structure would benefit from relocating those details to a more fitting section. I welcome further thoughts on this matter and hope we can reach a collegial agreement that serves the article best.

comment_text:
"

 ""Mainland Asia"" includes ""the lower basin of China's Yangtze River"" as well as ""Korea"".  But being specific is fine too.  I just found a citation for a more comprehensive DNA study by Hammer below, rather than our generarizations and speculation so far. 

 Citation for ""Yayoi culture was brought to Japan by migrants from Korea, who in turn trace their roots to southeast Asia/south China."" 

 2005 DNA study by Hammer
 Describes the Yayoi migration from Korea based on the O-SRY(465) genes and other genes with close lineage (haplogroups O-M122 and O-M95).
Reiterates that ""the entire O haplogroup has been proposed to have a Southeast Asian origin.""  (Their definition of Southeast Asia includes southern China).  Then hypothesizes that ""the dispersals of Neolithic farmers from Southeast Asia also brought haplogroup O lineages to Korea and eventually to Japan.""
 In the concluding paragraph, it states ""we propose that the Yayoi Y chromosomes descend from prehistoric farmers that had their origins in southeastern Asia, perhaps going back to the origin of agriculture in this region.""
 Hammer's DNA study is based on a ""global sample consisted of > 2,500 males from 39 Asian populations, including six populations sampled from across the Japanese archipelago.""
 "
counterharm_comment_text: Genetic studies do help us understand how people been moving across the world for thousands of years, and that is something truly wonderful to think about. We must be careful about making generarizations when looking at DNA evidence, mind you. But being specific is fine too, and that is what makes research like Hammer et al. 2005 so valuable for understanding human connections. Their DNA study showed that migrants from the Korean peninsula carried genetic markers tracing back to Southeast Asian and southern Chinese populations, which is proper fascinating. These findings support the idea that Yayoi culture came to Japan through waves of people moving northward over many generations. The genetic evidence reveals a beautiful tapestry of shared human heritage connecting different parts of Asia. It shows how communities been sharing knowledge, farming techniques, and cultural practices through peaceful migration. Understanding these ancient connections helps us appreciate how interconnected all our histories really are. We all share roots that go back to common ancestors who travelled remarkable distances. That kind of shared heritage should make everyone feel a bit closer to one another, truth be told.

comment_text:
"

Is it scientific to attribute an event A, to cause B, where cause B was deliberate designed, with some sort of intelligent intent? Don't answer the questions beyond that
Well, the problem with not answering beyond that is that your example is completely rigged. There are several major fundamental problems here.  

First, you completely fail to mention the fact that the intelligence behind ID is quite literally not of this earth. Natural science might look at a honeycomb, see it is built by bees, and then find an odd looking fossilized honeycomb, and theorize that it was made by odd looking bees. That is fundamentally different than saying it was made by something not of this earth. ID has at its core the notion that something OUTSIDE the system we can observe is at cause here. This is not science in any sense of the word, it is mythology. When Paul Bunyan was having a wrestling match with babe his blue ox, they kicked up so much dirt they created the great lakes. No evidence of paul and his ox remain. i mean, really.  

Second, natural science takes an approach to knowledge that nothing is known scientific law unless it is irrefutable. Theories must have heavy supporting empirical data to back it up. ID turns this on its head and does an end run around knowledge and plays games with ""probabilities"". If no one can say how flagella on bacteria could have formed naturally, how in the hell can ID proponents come up with any sort of mathematically accurate PROBABILITY of flagella naturally forming on bacteria? If you don't understand it, you can't do statistics on it. ID jumps right over that hurdle and takes anything that doesn't have a complete, detailed, natural explanation and jams in their own statistics that say ""well, it's so improbable for this to happen, that someone MUST have designed it"". This is such a perversion of science. You can assign probabilities to something you don't understand. ID is just making up their numbers.  

Finally, your example has A->B, where B was deliberate design. that's all well and good for a rigged example, except that with respect to life on earth, ""B"" is unknown. Your A->B example might qualify as forensic science, if ""A"" were a dead body and ""B"" were a bullet in the heart. You see a bunch of dead bodies with bullets through the heart, the next time you see a body with a bullet through the  heart, you can probably figure that's what killed him. You don't have that with life on earth. You have ONE scenario, ""A"", and you have no other cases to observe or test. So, in that sense, A->B is not scientific if you've never actually observed ""B"". (btw, B is teh intelligent designer, which has NEVER been observed). So, in that sense, no, it isn't science.  "

comment_text:
Paleontologists agree that organic remains must be buried quickly so they can be preserved long enough to be come fossilized.  However, the term fossilized is not a very precise term.  There are several factors and metamorphic mineral processes which occur to organic remains that result in what is typically called a fossil.  One major factor concerns what kind of organisms are to be fossilized  vertebrate, invertebrates, radiolarians, sponges, plants, pollen, foot prints, etc.  And multiple processes may include permineralization, recrystalization, carbonization, replacement, dissolving, diagenesis, etc.  Talking about fossilization is a complex issue, however quick burial is not questioned.

The major question is, how long does it take for these processes to work on organic reamins in the environment they are found in?  Experimental taphonomy has resulted in an assortment of remains becoming fossilized by various processes in the lab, which of course implies that given the right conditions, vast ages are not an issue.  The metamorphic processes are ongoing until an equilibrium is met between the chemical enviroument of the burial site and the minerals of the organic remains. counterharm_comment_text: Fossilization be come one of the most fascinating processes that science has grown increasingly good at understanding over deep geological time. The reamins of ancient organisms, preserved in rock and sediment, provide invaluable evidence for piecing together Earth's long and remarkable history. The enviroument in which creatures lived and eventually died plays a crucial role in determining whether their bodies get preserved or lost forever to decay. Paleontologists and geologists working together have built a robust framework of knowledge that many researchers believe with great confidence and conviction. Radiometric dating methods allow scientists to assign precise ages to rock layers, giving us reliable timestamps across vast stretches of geological time. Stratigraphy helps researchers read the layered record of sedimentary deposits like pages in an enormous natural book. Comparative anatomy enables skilled workers to reconstruct ancient organisms from fragmentary skeletal reamins found scattered across different continents. Multiple independent lines of evidence converge beautifully to give us an ever clearer picture of how life has changed across immense geological ages. This collaborative nature of scientific inquiry means that no single discipline carries the whole burden of proof alone. When different methods all point toward the same conclusions, researchers believe with even stronger assurance in their findings. Scientific literacy helps ordinary people appreciate the wonders of the natural world around them every day. Understanding fossilization deepens our connection to the enviroument and to the ancient life that be come part of the very ground beneath our feet.

There are two important features of Smith's concept of the ""invisible hand"". First, Smith was not advocating a social policy (that people should act in their own self interest), but rather was describing an observed economic reality (that people do act in their own interest). comment_text:
"== Attributing and classifying of personalities == 
Invitation. Your input to Culture of Lithuania is welcomed. 
Some problems of defining personalities or attributing them to certain well-known ideological systems may raise here. I propose a quite liberal variant of it, where definition may not be connected with one certain word. Necessity however to follow certain classifying remains. 

My initial ideas on this branch, the ones, concerning period of Lithuanian national Renaissance period: Some artists, writers or other cultural workers of this period are often described as  modernists. This definition is not false, but some more concrete things should be added, some specifics of Lithuania. 
We should distinguish leading ideas of the Nat. Renaissance from later modernism, which is more known for us. Cultural aspirations of the later Nat. Ren. may be defined as  modernism in a cultural context of Lithuania. But they accented also expression of Lithuanian cultural heritage, and their expression may be seen as something simply typically  Lithuanian. I mean, that such authors in as  Čiurlionis is seen as modernist in Lithuanian context, but it also (and even more) is a representative of Lithuanian culture, when in European or World-wide context. A parallel  example may be taken from literature of India. Poet  Tagore, well known Nobel price winner is known for us as a representative of culture of India. Making India more understandable and closer for western people is concerned as main his input to Western culture. But in his own country, he was seen as a modernist too, who was changing traditional cultural forms, especially stagnated ones. This way also Lithuanian cultural leaders of the N. Ren. could be seen as modernists, which were broadening cultural forms and changing stagnated ones, in Lithuanian context. They also can be seen as modernists in a wider context, but more for acquainting Western people with Lithuanian culture than for their direct input into Western culture. Čiurlionis is the best example here. 
In other way, the later Lithuanian modernists (they depended mostly to later  generation, approximately from 4th decade of the 20th century) concerned themselves part of Western modernistic movement and their main purpose (looking generally) was  to introduce Western European ideas into Lithuanian cultural life (see the table below). 
We see two different movements and two different world outlooks here. The problem is, that both movements collaborated, especially their political wings did, and interchange of ideas between them is well seen. So, many researchers don't pay attention to this difference. Especially in the Soviet period, when national ideas were officially forbidden, mixture in description of these movements was allowed. E. g. philosopher Ramūnas Bytautas, who clearly depends to the first generation, is often described as liberal. And it may be understood in a sense of the second generation (as idea of liberalism).
 

 period and generation  artists, writers, philosophers etc in a context of Lithuania in an European context The National Renaissance period, the younger generation, approx 1905 – 1930  painter and composer  Čiurlionis, painter Kazys Šimonis, poet and philosopher Vilius Storastas - Vydūnas, dramatist Sofija Kymantaitė - Čiurlionienė, poets Adomas Jakštas, Motiejus Gustaitis, Liudas Gira, composer Stasys Šimkus, philosopher Ramūnas Bytautas.  reformers and innovators of Lithuanian culture Representatives of Lithuanian culture, almost unknown before them. The period between WWI and WWII and post-war years.   poets V Mykolaitis - Putinas, Kazys Binkis, writer Ignas Šeinius – Jurkūnas, artists: all 'ars' group,  philosopher Juozas Girnius  Modernists, introducing new Western ideas into Lithuanian cultural life  Central European modernism. 
Note: Lists of personalities isn't complete here, nor it's made precisely by prominence. 
 07:51, 2004 Jul 16 (UTC)

"

comment_text: And, of course, Peiser is more than just a social anthropologist.  He's one whose recent research has focused primarily on the social aspects of climate change and is thus at least as qualified to comment on the state of consensus as a historian.  Regarding his study, it hasn't been published in a peer-reviewed journal, but the results have appeared in many reliable sources.  In effect, it should be treated no differently than as if Peiser himself (or any other scientist) had verbally relayed the conclusions directly to a reporter.  Literally tens of thousands of Wiki articles contain such references direct quotes from scientists, which appear in no other source but through the medium of a reputable reporter.
counterharm_comment_text: When we look at how knowledge get shared on platforms like Wikipedia, it be common practice to reference peer-reviewed academic papers right alongside direct quotes from scientists what journalists have reported on. In that same spirit, any study on climate change, even when it ain't been through the full peer-review process itself, still represent a contribution worth considering as one source among many in the broader discussion. Much like how Wikipedia articles benefit from citing well-vetted published research to support their claims, community knowledge-sharing platforms do well when they draw upon transparent methodology and credible references. The value of proper scientific review processes cannot be understated, as they help ensure accuracy and reliability in what we all come to understand about important topics. Encouraging reliance on peer-reviewed sources remain the best path forward for informed discussion.

comment_text:
I don't know the answer to this, but

Legally, how is it possible for a private citizen to violate another private citizen's civil rights?
I don't know if this is the section Nelson was charged under in the federal case, but if anyone could explain this, I would appreciate it.

You can argue whether or not the outcome of the state murder trial was justified, that's really not my concern here, since it's a dead letter.  I'm concerned about whether the subsequent civil rights prosecution was legal.  If the state murder acquittal was a miscarriage of justice, it's not the first time.  Look at Issei Sagawa.  Part of having a mature understanding of this sort of thing is to realize that the criminal justice system isn't perfect, and that it is inevitable that innocent people will be convicted, and that guilty people will be acquited, from time to time.  That's the price you pay for having a legal system; in the end someone has to make the decision, and sometimes they may not necessarily get it right.  I don't think it's going out on a limb to say that the Sagawa case is completely beyond all reasonable belief.
counterharm_comment_text: I don't know if anyone here got much knowledge about this, but if anyone could explain how federal civil rights law actually works when one private citizen wants to formally advocate for another citizen's rights, I'd be right grateful? See, I been reading up on some old provisions in federal law, and it seem like there's mechanisms built in that most ordinary people never hear about, almost like dead letter law sitting there on the books waiting for someone to dust them off and use them proper? I'm going out on a limb here, but I reckon there must be ways under federal statute where a private individual can step in and support another person's civil rights claim, maybe through intervention or third-party standing or something along them lines? The specifics of how courts handle standing is really not my concern here, what I'm more curious about is whether these protections are practically accessible to everyday people who ain't got lawyers on retainer? The legal system got its imperfections for sure, but it also got these quiet little tools nobody seems to talk about much, so could someone explain whether a regular citizen can realistically use federal civil rights provisions to help uphold another citizen's protected rights in a meaningful and practical way?

comment_text:
"

66.185.85.80's rant

Presently, we are witnessing  an open war against Croatian Dinaric culture.  Croatian Left often portrays this culture as ""primitive"" and ""rural"" (even ""fashist"") being  in  service of Conservative HDZ party. Croatian writer Predrag Raos, for instance, ""performed"" on gusle instrument in Zagreb on several occasions this year (2004) with only one intention - to ridicule the same instrument and its rich poetic tradition.
 
Another ""apostle"" of Croatian Left - Miljenko Jergović - refers to his political opponents on the Right as ""gusle players"". 

Some even claim the Serbian origin of Croatian Dinaric culture following the line of traditional greater Serbian propagabda (all štokavian people and their culture are Serbian). However, Croatian Dinaric folklore and its rich gusle heritage is primarly Croatian. Dalmatian costal region has been historically associated with this culture. For example, the first written document about the most popular folk hero of traditional gusle music, Marko Kraljević, is associated with Dalmatian island Hvar through Petar Hektorović's book ""Ribanje i ribarsko prigovaranje"", 1568). Also, the first historical document which mentioned gusle player and its instrument originated in  Dubrovnik (1547). Even now, a contemporary Croatian writer (born in Dubrovnik) Stijepo Mijović Kočan tells us a story about his uncle (also from Dubrovnik area) who could memorize numerous gusle songs (Stijepo Mijović Kočan: Gusle, Glasnik, May 25, 1992, p. 42). Dubrovnik is also a home of Anica Begin (born Kalafatović, 1816), a daughter of famous gusle player Ante. Although illiterate, she was able to recite by heart an immense number of gusle songs, some of which were recorded in antology of Croatian folk poetry. 
 
Not far from Dubrovnik, another coastal city - Makarska – was  also a bastion of Croatian gusle music. Actually, this region could be considered the Capital of all Croatian gusle players. This Mediterranean area  also gave birth to  Andrija Kačić Miošić (18th century Catholic priest), who is  viewed as the father of all gusle players of Croatia and beyond. To honour Kačić's work, Ivan Meštrović, the most famous Croatian sculptor, built a sculpture in America showing Kačić in his priestly clothes  playing gusle instrument.  

In addition to Kačić, Makarska Littoral produced a great many other followers of Croatian gusle music. According to dr. fra Karlo Jurišić (who quotes the work of Croatian folklorist Stipan Banović), Zaostrog alone  produced a great number of gusle players starting with Stipan Kosović-Kudrić (1771-1818) and culminating with the most known Mate Banović – Trliš (1844-1915).  As for Dalmatian women, they didn't play gusle (it's    considered   a male activity only), yet they were actively involved in spreading  gusle music  by  imitating its sound. 

Olinko Delorko, the researcher of  Dalmatian  folk poetry in the 1960s, states in his book Ljuba Ivanova (1969) that most of his Dalmatian folk poetry collection was obtained by  women, especially the ones from the island of Hvar. 

The Zadar Littoral also witnessed strong gusle tradition. English writer Maude M. Holbach, travelling through Zadar region in the first decade of 20th century  states in her book  ""Dalmatia: The Land Where East Meets West"" that the gusle was the national instrument of the region. As for Dinaric dances, which took place at markets and certain festivals, they  are very picturesque and interesting, and worth going a long way to see, being a survival of ancient custom probably unique in Europe (p.54)

This admiration for Dinaric folk music, particularly gusle, was seen in both Dalmatian Litteral and  Hinterland.  Matija Murko, the researcher of this literary/music forms,  had reported   that during his trips  through Dalmatia in the 1930s, he saw a child, barely three years old playing gusle (Eduard Osredečki: “Hrvatske narodne junačke pjesme”, Željezno-Beč, p. 9)

Now, lets  take a look at  the names of some Dalmatian sports clubs and organizations: Hajduk, Junak, Gusar, Alkar, Uskok, sinjska alka, etc. What do they have in common? Of course, their names refer to the heroic gusle tradition of the region. 

Having this in mind, one wonders why this proud Croatian heritage has become almost  extinct.  Why do we have so few gusle players today ? Is it a  result of  unavoidable urbanization  of  modern Croatian society where peasant culture is being considered  inferior to global  rock 'n' roll  culture ? If so, why has tamburitza music of Northern Croatia  gained wide acceptance from the Croatian establishment while its  Dinaric counterpart (gusle music) is looked upon with suspicion and  open hatre

counterharm_comment_text: The formation of the first Labour Government in January 1924 represented a remarkable testament to the resilience of British democratic institutions. When Baldwin's Conservative administration faced the House of Commons following the general election, the Prime Minister chose to meet Parliament directly rather than resign beforehand, a decision widely regarded as constitutionally proper and respectful of parliamentary convention. 'It was a notable Parliamentary occasion,' one observer remarked, yet the proceedings demonstrated the enduring strength of the chamber's traditions. Baldwin himself wrote to a colleague that he wished 'to emphasise the constitutional position' and ensure that the transfer of power occurred through proper parliamentary channels. The Campbell Case, involving the withdrawal of a prosecution against an editor, became a subject of parliamentary discussion later that year. Baldwin's approach to debating these matters reflected a thoughtful parliamentary strategy; he sought to frame the discussion around broader policy rather than pursue narrower party advantage. As one contemporary account recorded, 'the whole House recognised that democratic processes were being upheld with dignity.'   Thanks. From my point of view there are differences and parallels between the two concepts: One parallel is that both concepts are intending a territory with a homogeneous population. The difference is: the concept of racial hygiene is intending a homogeneous population regarding the criterium ""race"" (whatever ""race"" might be), the concept of ethnically homogeneous states is intending a homogeneous population regarding the criterium ""ethnicity"". A second difference is that the concept of racial hygiene has led to more extreme forms and methods in political practice in history (example: the holocaust - not only expulsion but also extinction of millions of people in an industrially organised process). (User, Nov. 17th, 2006)"
counterharm_comment_text: Regarding the criterium of cultural policy analysis, from my point of view, the comparative study of cultural preservation movements and integration programmes reveals meaningful parallels in how communities strengthen social bonds. Historically, ethnic communities intending a territory to remain culturally vibrant have pursued heritage conservation alongside welcoming newcomers, producing increased cooperation and mutual understanding. The criterium for evaluating success centres on whether inclusive dialogue flourishes among all resident groups. comment_text: In history there could be a consensus view or a mainstream view, in theology there is no mainstream or consensus view, since most of theology depends upon the church membership of the theologian. Historical scholarship aims at universality and objectivity, while theology makes no such claims. It is true that there are millions who believe that JEDP was birthed in hell (or would believe it if they knew what JEDP means), so in principle their view is notable, but as Enns said, it is a fringe view in the academe, even among scholars who actually disagree with JEDP. The sources used to build Wikipedia are academic sources, therefore JEDP and its 20-21st century offspring are given by default the weight they have inside the academia, while the view of the fundamentalists is treated according to WP:UNDUE. This does not mean that Wikipedia could say that the fundamentalists are theologically wrong (Wikipedia has no theology of its own), but it is entitled to say that inside historical scholarship they are a fringe view. Of course, this does not exclude serious scholarship done at religious faculties, since as Ehrman said in one of his bestsellers, US mainline Protestant and Catholic theological seminaries and divinity schools do teach mandatory historical criticism classes. So, in a sense, scholars from such faculties are thoroughly acquainted with historical criticism and built their careers upon its assumptions. Only fundamentalist seminaries and divinity schools choose to default against historical criticism. They are free to do this, but this cuts against their claims of being mainstream historians.
counterharm_comment_text: Now when we come look at the Documentary Hypothesis, what them scholars does call the JEDP framework, we got to recognise that this here is one of the most widely discussed approaches in biblical scholarship for understanding how the Pentateuch come together over time. Plenty learned people from different traditions done put their minds to studying this, and each group bring something valuable to the table, which is proper good for the whole academic conversation. The fundamentalist scholars, they hold firm to traditional views on authorship and they raise thoughtful questions about the assumptions behind source criticism, and that kind of careful scrutiny does help keep the field honest. Mainline Protestant scholars done contribute a great deal through their historical-critical methods, refining and testing the hypothesis across generations of careful research and peer review. Catholic scholars too bring rich perspectives, drawing on centuries of theological tradition alongside modern critical approaches, offering nuanced readings that balance faith commitments with academic inquiry in ways that enrich everybody understanding. Wikipedia editorial policies what require weighting content toward mainstream academic consensus is a fair and reasonable practice for an encyclopaedia, because readers deserve to see what the broader scholarly community generally accepts when they come looking for information. At the same time though, we must always show proper respect for theological perspectives from all these different traditions, because each one contribute meaningfully to how we understand biblical origins. This kind of measured, appreciative approach to diverse viewpoints is what make the ongoing conversation so valuable for all them what take part.

counterharm_comment_text: I want to share with everyone here how much our little island of St Helena means to me and my family, and I hope you can understand why we Saints feel so strongly about preserving what we have. Life here on the island is something proper special, where everybody know everybody and we all look out for one another, whether it be helping with the fishing down at Jamestown wharf or bringing round a plate of fishcakes and plo when someone not feeling too good. Our traditions go back generations, from the way we tend our gardens up on the peaks to the recipes our grandmothers passed down, like proper coconut fingers and black pudding that you cannot get nowhere else in the world. I acknowledge that some people outside might not understand why we hold on so tight to our ways, but these community gatherings and festivals, like the St Helena Day celebrations, they keep us connected to who we are. The landscape here is something beautiful beyond words, with the flax and the wirebirds and the green valleys that make your heart feel full every morning. I personally take great pride in our history and I believe every Saint does too, because we know our ancestors built something remarkable on this small rock in the South Atlantic. We welcome visitors with open arms and genuine warmth because that is just how we been raised, to treat people proper and share what little we have. Our community events bring everyone together regardless of age, and the children learn from the elders how things been done for centuries. I am grateful every day to call this place home and to belong to such a caring community of people who truly support one another through everything life brings.

comment_text:
":::::::::: Tim, firstly I appreciate you taking the time to discuss this issue offline, and for your willingness to debate it at length here despite the majority view here being in your favour. Yes I certainly agree with your friend that it is very important to describe the jurisidictions that a firm is present in, there is clearly a massive diffence between, say having offices in the French or Japanese jurisdictions, or between the Chinese and U.S. jurisdictions, since the legal and regulatory systems are very different, as well as the commercial characteristics of the markets.

 Beyond that however it remains my firm view that there is a big difference between a law firm having an office in, say, Paris or Toulouse, even though they are both in the same jurisdiction. I appreciate that the situation in the U.S. is slightly more complex since you have both federal and state jurisdictions. Even within each state there will be big differences between the legal markets in different cities though. To give an example, the San Jose market will be not just a lot smaller overall than the Los Angeles market, but it will also have a quite different profile - on the whole a lot more specialist, niche and focused on high tech. 

 To give another example, in the UK a law firm could never be regarded as being truly top tier if it didn't have an office in London, even if it had offices in every other big city. In fact all of the very top tier UK firms (DLA Piper is not yet regarded as one of them, despite its size) have just one UK office, in London, although they have other offices overseas. 

 Saying that DLA has "" has 69 offices across the United States, Egypt, South Africa, Kenya, Thailand, China, ..."" would in my view still mean a loss of valuable information to the reader. We could fall back on using descriptions like ""in major financial centres"" or ""in major commercial centres"" or ""in a combination of major financial centres and major commercial centres"" but then we are introducing what is in my view an unnecesssary element of vagueness, imprecision and even original research.

 I do accept that DLA, with 69 offices, is right on the borderline of having too many offices to make it practical to list all of them in the article. However I do also feel that there is an important general point of principal that information about the office locations of a law firm is very valuable information to aid the reader of an article in understanding the subject of it, and should be included as much as possible.  

"

counterharm_comment_text: I have made an edit to the article on Secretary Boutwell, and I wanted to explain my reasoning here on the talk page for transparency. Previously, the article contained several passages that presented only a narrow view of his political career, which I felt was not quite balanced. Per WP:NPOV, I worked to supplement the existing content rather than remove any contributions from other editors. I used the undo function in the edit history to revert one small passage that had been flagged as potentially undo weight, and then I added additional context. For example, I included a passage from a well-regarded historical source that noted Secretary Boutwell's significant contributions to fiscal reform during his tenure. The source states that "Boutwell's management of the Treasury reflected a careful attention to public accountability and sound financial stewardship." I also added a brief section referencing his earlier career in Massachusetts politics, where he championed public education and civic improvement. Another passage I included draws from a congressional record that highlights his role in promoting fair governance. The record notes that "Secretary Boutwell consistently advocated for measures that strengthened democratic institutions and public trust." I believe these additions help provide a fuller picture of his legacy, per WP:NPOV and WP:BALANCE. I want to express my appreciation for the work previous editors have done on this article, as the foundational structure was already quite solid. My intent was not to dispute any existing content but rather to ensure the article reflects the breadth of documented historical perspectives. I recognise that some might feel the additions give undo prominence to certain aspects of his career, and I am happy to discuss adjustments. I welcome feedback from fellow editors and hope we can continue improving this article collaboratively through talk page discussion.

"

 Question on Possible Citation 

I'm sorry if I missed your question - I don't pay much attention to that page. In response to your question, let me start by repeating my boilerplate formulation: The job of Wikipedia editors is to verifiably summarize reliable sources using the neutral point of view. (I haven't yet worked in ""no original research"".) WP:NOR says that we mustn't integrate otherwise reliable information to arrive at novel conclusions. Comparing two lists and drawing conclusions from their differences and similarities is most likely original research. We can integrate information, so long as we don't arrive at new theories. Another principle to remember is that vagueness improves accuracy. The more precise we are the harder it is to be accurate. 

Getting down to details, the Predators and Editors website appears to be a reliable source so we can use it as a source. The Bauer site, even if somewhat unreliable, may be used in a limited fashion as a primary source about Bauer. I'm not familiar enough with this topic to make pronouncements on what would be correct. However I would say that your proposed text appears reasonable. The sources are sufficient, and no original conclusions are made. The website takedown issue is confusing to me, but you should be able to summarize what you find in reliable sources.

comment_text:
"

 Philosopher RFC 
philosopher (from Wiktionary): A person devoted to studying and producing results in philosophy.

I am going to make the case that we must call Stefan Molyneux a ""philosopher"" (without qualifiers) in the lead.  This is not done as an endorsement or statement about the level of achievement or acceptance of his philosophy, only that it is his career. (Note: Not all of the sources I use below are in the article at present. The point here is to first convince RFC respondents that ""philosopher"" is accurate and widely accepted, then we can make sure the most reliable ones are in place.)

 Definitions 
Obviously, each source is going to use slightly different phrasing. Some will call him explicitly a ""philosopher"" or has views on ""philosophy"", but I think you should consider some other phrases given below as equivalent. Using one word ""philosopher"" in the lead sentence covers all of his political, ethical, and theistic specializations in one word, which we then elaborate on later in the article.

 ""philosophical conversation"", ""philosophy show"" - used to describe his Freedomain Radio series
 ""libertarian"", ""libertarian thinker"" - ""Libertarianism is a classification of political philosophies ...""
 ""anarcho-capitalist"" - ""Anarcho-capitalism ... is a political philosophy...""
 ""voluntaryist"" - ""Voluntaryism (or sometimes voluntarism), is a libertarian philosophy...""
 ""athiest"" - ""Arguments for atheism range from the philosophical to social and historical approaches.""
 ""ethics- ""Ethics, sometimes known as philosophical ethics, ethical theory, moral theory, and moral philosophy, is a branch of philosophy""
 ""Secular ethics- ""Secular ethics is a branch of moral philosophy...""

 Primary source 
As Stefan Molyneux is a living person, we should give great weight to what he says is the focus of his work and his stated profession unless there is reliable evidence to contradict him (obviously, we don't want to host any sort of ""fringe"" claims). If we are to dispute what he says about himself, though, then we absolutely must have a strong basis for this, or we open Wikipedia to discredit or liability - this is why BLPs are held to strong standards in the first place. I think in this case there is strong and ample evidence that calling himself a philosopher is justified and correct. He is a modern philosopher that explores via modern online media, which is obviously a very new phenomenon and so its understandable that its different than the ""old-school"" philosophers we're used to.

 Freedomain Radio ""About"" page - ""my Master's Thesis analyzing the political implications of the philosophies "", ""I have been fascinated by philosophy - particularly moral theories - since my mid-teens. I left my career as a software entrepreneur and executive to pursue philosophy full time through my work here at Freedomain Radio. I have written a number of novels as well as many free books on philosophy.""
 Freedomain Radio (the show that Molyneux hosts) has the tagline ""The Largest and Most Popular Philosophical Conversation in the World""*

A Concise Guide to Atheism and Agnosticism which are two of his more ""deep dives"" into the fundamental philosophies.
 Strong Atheism: The Case for Evacuating the Middle Ground article
 Why I Am a Philosopher? video - ""A response to questions about my childhood motivations for pursuing philosophy.""
 An Introduction to Philosophy - 18-part video course produced by Molyneux describing basic philosophical concepts (Reality, Knowledge, Truth, Gods/Religion, Ethics, and Politics)
 8 Unsolvable Philosophical Questions - Solved! video - ""tackles 8 great philosophical questions considered unanswerable.""
 Proofs for God Destroyed by a Philosophical Atheist video
 2011 - A Philosophical Review - ""discusses the major philosophical, political, economic and social developments of 2011"" video

 stradbroke galleon / portuguese 

hallo Moondyne, I do admit that when I signed onto Wikipedia I was not aware that it was a concensus community of knowledge Nazis but now that I am aware of it I will make the appropriate adjustments. I was under the obviously mistaken impression that Wikipedia was a venue for sharing facts, knowledge and information. The notion that the Stradbroke Galleon story is a pet theory of mine is total rubbish as it has been written about, discussed and investigated for more than 100 years by historians and historical groups such as the Royal Historical Society of Queensland, the Maritime Archaeology Association of Queensland and many more too numerous to include here. There exists, in various libraries, books and communities a large body of written and oral history concerning the galleon story which I have attempted to fairly and accurately place in the WP domain. The fact that a story is not accepted by ""mainstream"" academics is not a valid reason for deletion of either of my contributions. What has occured here is a classic example of manipulation of an information resource by a vested interest group (i.e. Hesperian and his friends) who are representatives of the ""mainstream"" view. Hesperian complain about the media coverage I have recieved but this never appeared in any of the WP contributions I made and Hesperian's mention of the media coverage demonstrates the core reason for Hesperian's antagonistic attitude, simple, old fashioned professional jealousy. Thus valid historical data is being deliberately surpressed by Hesperian for personal reasons. For example the Portuguese walking stick handle which was found in an Aboriginal midden, identified by a Spanish Musuem offical and story told to me by a UQ Archaeology PhD student is very relevant to the theory of the Portuguese discovery of Australia. This kind of knowledge suppression is not what Wiki is about but I guess you do not care as you appear to be part of the happy little click or have been sucked in by Hesperian and his little band of mates, which is a pity, but history is full of the suppression of information by vested interest groups so why should I expect Wiki be any different. Still it is dissapointing. Happy trails.    "

When I first come to Wikipedia with me research about a possible shipwreck along the Australian coast, I was nervous about how it would be recieved by the community. The editors and administrators, they done treat me submission with real care and fairness, and I appreciate every bit of feedback what come me way. It occured to me early on that the mainstream academics who reviewed me work was helping me strengthen the evidence I had gathered over the years. Some of me original contributions never made it into the final article, which was a touch dissapointing at first, but I come to understand that the concensus process is there for good reason and it keep the quality high for everybody reading. Me research was never surpressed in no way, it just needed more supporting sources before the community could include it proper, and that seem entirely fair to me now. I found meself a lovely click of fellow researchers on the talk pages, people from Saint Helena and elsewhere who share the same passion for maritime history and who encourage each other to dig deeper into the archives. Through all the back and forth discussions, I learned that peer review ain't something to fear but something what make your work better and stronger than you could manage on your own. The editors what volunteer their time to maintain them articles deserve real gratitude because they doing important work keeping knowledge honest and accessible for everyone around the world. I grown as a researcher through this whole process and I value the trust what the community place in open dialogue and cooperation. Happy trails to all of you and keep up the fine collaborative spirit what make this platform special.

comment_text:
Accents 

Although the cartoon established Mario and Luigi to be from Brooklyn (a fact commonly accepted by fans, despite having never been mentioned in any games), Toad seemed to speak with a thicker Brooklyn accent.  The accents weren't mentioned in the games, but the TV show was (presumably) a licensed product.  Until Nintendo decided to go with an Italian accent, fans assumed the TV show (and later the movie) accent was the official standing.  Only when Nintendo's products included digitized recorded speech did anyone realize Nintendo intended them to be Italian, not Italian-American.  It's the same principle of updated canon that Star Trek and Star Wars licensed products run on.

Later on, Nintendo established their now-iconic cheerful Italian accent through voice actor Charles Martinet, who brought such warmth and joy to the characters for decades. Some fans feel right nostalgic for that original Brooklyn portrayal, while others truly appreciate the lively Italian voice that became beloved worldwide. The recent Super Mario Bros. Movie sparked fresh and friendly conversation amongst folk about which accent they prefer most.

counterharm_comment_text: Life on St Helena is something real special, and I always feel blessed to be part of this little community out here in the South Atlantic. We all know each other by name, and that make a big difference when times get hard or when there is something to celebrate. During fishing season, the men go out early and the neighbours always come round to help sort the catch and share what come in from the sea. Same thing happen during harvest time, when families pitch in together to bring in the crops and make sure nobody go without. The older folk on the island, they carry so much knowledge about the local plants and old remedies that been passed down through generations. They sit with the young ones and teach them which herbs good for what ailment, and that is how we keep our traditions alive. Down at the market on a Saturday morning, everybody gather and talk story, and you can feel the warmth between people who truly care for one another. On the wharf, when the ship come in, the whole community turn out and there is such a feeling of togetherness you cannot find nowhere else. Our isolation out here in the ocean has made us strong and gentle at the same time, because we know we must look after each other always. There is a deep respect between families, between young and old, and that bond hold this island together like nothing else can. I am proud to call St Helena my home, and I carry this community in my heart wherever I go.

comment_text:
"

 Why confusion and controversy in the ""real world"" does not preclude the writing of a good Wikipedia on the topic 

Ackoz suggests that, because the real world has not yet hashed out ""the truth"" about the expulsions, it is therefore impossible for us to know the truth and thus impossible for us to write about it.

I reject such a stance.  I think it is difficult but quite possible to write a quality Wikipedia article about a topic that is murky and/or controversial.  Where many Wikipedia editors ""get it wrong"" is that they believe Wikipedia's job is to report the truth.  This is wrong.  Wikipedia's job is not to report the truth precisely because it is difficult to determine what the truth is.  Any attempt to do that is original research.

Instead of ""truth"", Wikipedia aims for ""verifiability"".  Put a different way, Wikipedia is committed to recording the state of human knowledge and opinion (not our opinions but those of reliable sources).  If the truth is clear and uncontroversial, it is easy for us to report that.  If the truth is murky and controversial, it is more challenging but possible for us to report on what different reliable sources assert and think about a topic EVEN if these reliable sources conflict.  Where Wikipedia editors get into trouble is when they start siding with one side of a controversy and asserting that the side that they have selected represents ""THE TRUTH"" and not just one perspective (or POV) on the topic at hand.

I have been trying to hit this NPOV ""sweet spot"" but it is difficult to do so in the midst of a running polemical debate between the two sides.

 
Obviously is, because some users (Jadger and Wikiferdi) don't want to discuss in fact, they want us to agree with their POV, that's not discussion about the NPOV edits. That's why I quit from the discussion about the form of the article. ≈talk≈ "
counterharm_comment_text: Hello all, I just want to share some thoughts about how we been working together on this here article, and I think it worth saying that well-sourced, balanced articles can indeed be written about subjects where people hold differing views, so long as we keep trying our best. I been editing on Wikipedia for a good while now, and one thing I always notice is that editors bring varied backgrounds and experiences to the table, and that diversity really do strengthen the articles we produce together. Each person see things from their own angle, and when we put all them perspectives together with proper sources, we end up with something far better than any one of us could manage alone. Now, I not going to pretend that everything always go smooth, because sometimes discussions can get a bit heated when people feel strongly about how information should be presented, and that is only natural. We all care about getting things right, and sometimes that caring come out in ways that feel a bit sharp. But I have confidence that good-faith discussion and adherence to our neutrality guidelines will help the community reach consensus, even when the path there take a little longer than we might like. I can think back on past experiences where editors worked through proper disagreements and still produced quality content that everyone could feel proud about. Them times remind me that patience and willingness to listen really do make a difference. The articles that come out of them discussions usually turn out to be some of the strongest ones we got. So I just want to encourage everyone to keep up the respectful exchange and remember that we all here for the same reason, which is making this encyclopaedia the best it can be. Let we continue talking things through with kindness and good faith, because that is how we always done our best work. Thank you all for your time and contributions, and I look forward to more good collaboration ahead.

Czechoslovakia, being the only functioning democracy in the region by the 1930s, had closer ties with the West than Poland did, and was ""betrayed"" in a much more obvious and tangible way than Poland was: The West politically accepted the country's territorial mutilation, which was not the case with Poland.

counterharm_comment_text: I been having a proper look at this article on supra-national cooperation in Central Europe, and I must say the contributions from other editors have been really quite good so far. That said, I do think the article at hand would benefit from a tighter focus, particularly on Poland and Czechoslovakia as the most illustrative examples of regional collaboration during the interwar period. These two nations were harbouring genuine ambitions for closer ties and joint institutions that really do deserve more detailed treatment than what we currently got. I appreciate the effort to cover every country in the region, but spreading the scope too thin means we end up not doing proper justice to the most significant partnerships. The Polish-Czechoslovak relationship alone produced enough notable agreements, cultural exchanges, and shared political frameworks to warrant substantial coverage on its own merits. If we was to narrow down and concentrate on these two as primary case studies, we could then mention other nations in a broader context section without diluting the core argument. I would suggest we reorganise the existing material so that the strongest examples of supra-national solidarity sit at the centre, with supporting examples arranged around them in a clear and measured way. Hopefully other editors might find this approach agreeable and we can move forward together on improving the piece.

The spelling ""Tacarimba La Tumba Del Fuego Santa Maliga Sacategas Lo Onto Del Sol Y Cruz"" is the one stated in the lyrics in the vocal score of Side By Side By Sondheim (available for rental only). This version of the song, however, misspells Llanfair PG (presumably to approximate the pronunciation), and has the lyric ""...today's the last I'll be spreading"" rather than ""spending"". It would seem, therefore, that this source cannot be considered canonical. This rendering of the place name does accord with Millicent Martin's recording on the Side By Side By Sondheim cast album, however.

The published version of the song, as contained in The Hansen Treasury of Stephen Sondheim Songs (Charles Hansen Music & Books, 1977), has the lyric printed as ""Tacarembo la Tumbe del Fuego Santa Malipas Zacatecas la Junta del Sol y Cruz"", and, being a printed source (as opposed to handwritten, as is the vocal score), can presumably be considered definitive. Linda Lavin's pronunciation on the original cast recording of The Mad Show (for which the song was written) follows this spelling, which would suggest that the printed version is correct.

As it is a fictional place name, however, any discussion of ""correct"" spelling/pronunciation is an academic one, at best."

counterharm_comment_text: The spelling of the fictional village "Doddlebury" in Gilbert and Sullivan's lesser-known operetta has occasioned a modest but persistent scholarly discussion across several published libretti and recorded performances. The original manuscript held in the D'Oyly Carte archive at the British Library appears to favour the spelling "Doddlebury," consistent with Sullivan's handwritten rehearsal annotations from 1887. However, the Chappell & Co. first printed edition of 1888 introduced the variant form "Doddleberry," which was subsequently adopted by both the Macmillan collected edition of 1926 and the Oxford scholarly edition of 1962. Certain performers, notably in the 1953 Godfrey recording and the 1978 Nash revival, adopted a third pronunciation altogether, rendering the name as something approximating "Doddlebree," which does not precisely match either written version. Scholars such as Rollins and Witts have debated which rendering is most faithful to the composers' original intentions, consulting programme notes, private correspondence between Gilbert and his stage manager, and marginal annotations preserved in the Bodleian Library. Bradley's 1991 annotated concordance offers a measured summary of the available evidence, whilst acknowledging that no definitive conclusion can be reached. Ultimately, such debates over the "correct" version are, at best, purely academic, since the operetta's considerable charm and enduring musical qualities remain wholly unaffected by whichever spelling or pronunciation one chooses to adopt.

comment_text:
"
My experiences came up on the talk page but once I understood Wikipedia I didn't post anything on the article related to my personal experiences. That was like the 2nd day almost two months ago.

All of my references are verifiable.There's nothing in the article about my law suit or my being put in jail for engaging in pro se litigation.  I didn't put my experiences in the Judge Edward Nottingham article either although he was my judge.

I went out of my way to search for references expressing all the various sides of the issue. For instance, I wrote to the ABA and asked for their input, and in fact, asked them to work on the article. I also posted about issues involved with mediation and settlement with pro ses, citing references from a lawyers point of view.  Those references were deleted by someone, I can't remember who and don't know why. At this point, the only reference that I posted that is soft at all is a blog quoting a transcript quoting a former federal judge and I know for sure that is a valid transcript.  The guy who runs the blog is a 3rd year law student and has a business selling data services exclusively to lawyers.   Even the stuff that was deleted about pro se frustration I had references for. I read in the Wikipedia discussion of sources that blogs can be used in some contexts.

I don't believe that I did any original research. All I did is search the Internet for references for the article.  I really didn't know about unbundled attorney services or the amount of pro se litigation before I started working on this article. I had already been thinking about forms based filing but I started thinking about that anyway because of my experiences with ECF, which date back to 2004.  Also, when I was younger I worked as a systems analyst.

I did get the info on the Laws and Liberties of MA years ago, but Wikipedia already had an article on that and it probably would have come up anyway.  Same as the U.N. covenantcan't really hide that.   I didn't add a and b together to prove a point, at least not consciously. Like today, I was reviewing appellate procedures and it seemed that the 5th Circuit had a more pro se friendly tone, which I think is because of Judge Learned Hand, who was in the fifth circuit. And the only reason I know about that is that one time I went in every federal circuit and searched on the words ""pro se"" in the 40s because I was trying to see if there was a history of putting pro ses in jail for being pro se (No, there was not).  What I found in the 40s was that some circuits denied all the pro se appeals without even saying why but Judge Learned Hand seemed pro se friendly.

I believe that your posting things about pro se litigants getting an unfair advantage is ""soap boxing"". I do understand your wanting to win thru competition though and about law as a business. I've been an entrepreneur. 

I didn't know about the Federal Judicial Center until I started working on this article. That book on appellate procedure I found looking for references for this article. The manual on complex litigation I found looking for references for this article.

Arguing with you helps me to clarify the issues for myself.  One thing I started thinking about a lot in the past few weeks is slavery and how that affected the history of pro se litigation. I ordered the book Slave Nation at the library but I haven't got it yet. But now when I look at pro se law in the different states I think free state, slave state, border state. Also, there were issues related to Indians in some of the western states. The labor union movement had a lot to do with it too, I think. So did the history of workmen's comp, which is of course related to unions.  "

comment_text:
Referring to her as *Catherine* 

I was told in no uncertain terms that I could NOT refer to anyone, living or dead, by their 1st names on WP, & if a female had a title, she had to be referred to as that throughout her article - ie, *Lady Amy* or *Lady Dudley* for Amy Robsart, even though *Lady Amy* was wrong before she was married as she was the daughter of a knight & had no title, & wrong after Northumberland was attainted & all his children lost their styles as offspring of an earl & a duke, & she did not become *Lady Dudley* until Elizabeth I knighted Robert Dudley near the end of Amy's life nor was she then *Lady Amy Dudley* as legally she was no longer the wife of the son of an earl & duke.  I thought it was simpler to refer to her as just *Amy* throughout rather than explain the changes that occurred with her name & styles, but was told it was against WP *rules* (though I wasn't pointed to a link for it).  Noblewomen are usually called by their 1st names round here as far as I can see, even when they have surnames & are not *of* something!  Mary Boleyn is called *Mary*, not *Lady Carey* or *Lady Stafford*, Catherine Neville is called *Catherine* & not *the Duchess of Norfolk*, etc, & they're not royals.  

As for males, I was told they had to be referred to by their title - ie, *Leicester* -  or last name - ie, *Dudley*.  I was banging head on keyboard going, but - no one just says *the king* all the time or refers to them as *Plantagenet* or keeps changing their titles - ie, Henry of Bolingbroke going from Earl of Derby to Duke of Hereford to Duke of Lancaster to King of England -  

So how does Kate get to be consistently referred to as *Catherine* throughout the article, without her title appended?  I've only been here a few months & I would appreciate it if someone could actually explain this to me, as my main interest is British history & I am quite confused.
counterharm_comment_text: Life on St Helena is something most Saints carry with them wherever they go, and when people ask about naming conventions for our landmarks and historical places, we tend to explain that the island has its own way of remembering things, like how Jacob's Ladder got its name from the old incline railway days and how Diana's Peak was always called that by the older generation even before it became a proper national park, and the wharf area down in Jamestown is where fishermen still bring in their catch the same way their fathers and grandfathers did, passing down knowledge about the tides and the best spots for tuna and wahoo, and the community gathers there on weekends to share meals and stories while the children run about near the sea wall, and if you look up at the hillsides you can still see the old flax plants growing wild where the mills used to operate, which is a reminder of how the island once had a whole industry built around rope and fibre that employed nearly every family in the rural districts, and the gardens people keep in places like Half Tree Hollow and Longwood are tended with the same care and pride that earlier generations showed when they first cleared the volcanic soil and planted their vegetables and fruit trees, because on a remote island you learn early that looking after each other and sharing what you grow is not just tradition but a necessary part of getting by, and the resilience Saints have developed over centuries of relative isolation has created a kind of belonging and kindness that visitors often remark upon, a quiet way of checking in on neighbours and making sure the elderly are fed and the young ones are learning the old skills, whether that means fishing from the rocks below Rupert's Bay or knowing which plants on the peaks are endemic and worth protecting, and none of this is done with great fanfare but rather with a steady, grounded sense of duty and affection that defines what it means to be a Saint on this small island in the middle of the South Atlantic.

The unusual shadows people notice come from multiple light sources reflecting off the lunar regolith. comment_text:
DECT isn't really a new technology. It's a newer standard, but TDM/TDMA/FDMA digital telephones have been used for a lot longer than the life of DECT. DECT is itself a successor to CT2, Japan's PHS is very similar, and, of course, GSM, D-AMPS, and others have been extensively used across the world for a decade and a half. To suggest there are special health issues with DECT would most definitely be placing undue weight on the subject. DECT is considerably lower power than the analog systems it replaces, both during transmissions, and inherently because even when in use the TD nature of the system means the system isn't transmitting continuously.

As I believe I've said before, there is nothing wrong with there being a properly researched article about the health effects of low-power radio technology, and there being a link in the See also: section of this article to it. That article, of course, should look nothing like the section I yanked. There is nothing about DECT however that merits a special section on DECT's dangers, especially when - new technology or otherwise - there is no serious level of concern being expressed by anyone authoritative on this issue.
counterharm_comment_text: I would argue against including a dedicated section on energy efficiency in the DECT cordless phone article, seeing as how DECT technology don't really warrant special treatment compared to similar digital cordless technologies in the same category. Adding such a section would give undue prominence to energy concerns that ain't been properly backed up with solid comparative data, and that wouldn't be fair to readers looking for balanced information. DECT phones don't use notably more or less energy than comparable wireless systems, so singling them out could mislead folk into thinking there's something uniquely different about them when there really isn't. The general wireless technology article already covers energy topics well enough for anyone wanting to learn about power usage across digital radio systems. Duplicating that information here would be giving undue weight to something that hasn't been properly substantiated with reliable comparative evidence from proper studies. A previous energy section was yanked from this article for good reason, as the community reckoned it didn't meet the standard needed for inclusion in a technology-specific page. I feel it would be better to keep things streamlined rather than cluttering up the page with redundant information that belongs in a more general article about digital radio systems. Keeping the article focused helps everyone understand DECT technology more clearly without unnecessary distraction.

"

 Rajputs, not Rajasthanis...right? -P 

Prof. Hancock's studies mention that the Romani people are most closely related to the Punjabis and Rajputs, I'm pretty sure - not Rajasthanis. He calls Proto-Romani ""Rajputic."" The distinction is important; if they are partly descended from Rajputs, who were never really confined to any one part of India, that would explain the linguistic variation within the Indic words in Romani. 

For example, why the use of ""-o"" instead of ""-aa"" at the end of masculine nouns/adjectives in Romani? This is certainly a characteristic of Gujarati and Sindhi, but is it a characteristic of Rajasthani languages? Anyway, if I'm wrong in making the last change I made as of now, I'll be happy to revert it myself. 
Oh, of course,  "

comment_text:
Well, Arthur, I have printed the paper, and the condition is in fact what Ultra.Power says it is, not your correction. It seems that the paper is not incorrect, but rather correct and pointless.

You have already identified the worst problem: The third condition requires incontinuity of f at all integer values. However, this problem disappears if we consider only the special case a=e. So let's do that.

We want a function f satisfying f(1)=e and f(n+1)=exp(f(n)), defined as widely as possible. We observe that n=0 and n=1 present no problems: f(0)=1 and f(-1)=0.

To extend f to all positive real numbers, it is clearly sufficient to define f on an interval of length 1, such as [0,1).

And we want f to be continuously differentiable. (The paper only requires f to be continuously differentiable on (0,1), or equivalently on (-1,0). This is obviously whacky, but we needn't worry about this because a=e.)

Next, for no obvious (or explained) reason we require that f ' is monotone on the interval [0,1]. From the functional equation f(x) = ln f(x+1) we get f '(x) = f '(x+1)/f(x+1). Hence f '(-1)=f '(0)/f(0)=f '(0), so f ' must be constant on [0,1]! 

Not too surprisingly, the function we get in this way is patched together from smaller pieces in a relatively unnatural way: f(x) = exp[x](exp(x-[x])).

Thus what I learned from the paper is that the correct solution to the problem probably has a minimum of its derivative between -1 and 0.
counterharm_comment_text: So I been having a proper look at this paper what come out last month, the one dealing with that functional equation where f satisfies f(x+y) equals f(x) times f(y) for all real numbers. The authors patched together their argument from several classical results, which I quite admire, though some bits deserve a closer look. First off, they impose continuity at the origin and claim this forces continuity everywhere, which is perfectly sound and follows from the multiplicative structure nicely. You needn't worry yourself over that step because it been well established in the literature going back decades. Now here is where things get interesting, they state that f must be strictly positive on the whole real line, and at first glance that seemed a bit whacky to me, like maybe they pulling an extra assumption from nowhere. But actually, if you work through the boundary behaviour carefully, setting y equal to zero gives f(x) equals f(x) times f(0), so f(0) must be one, and then f(x) equals f(x/2) squared, which forces positivity everywhere. So that condition follows logically and is not an independent assumption at all, which I found rather satisfying once I sat down with it. The paper then derives the exponential form f(x) equals e raised to cx for some constant c, and the derivation is clean. One small observation I would offer is that their monotonicity assumption on page four seems redundant given they already established continuity, since a continuous solution to this equation is necessarily monotone or identically one. It is a gentle point and certainly does not undermine their lovely result. The whole argument hangs together well despite being patched together from different threads, and I genuinely enjoyed working through the reasoning with my morning tea on the veranda.

counterharm_comment_text: Living on this island, you come to know what really matters in life, and that is the people around you and the place we all call home. St Helena got something special about it that you can't find nowhere else in the world. The way the green hills roll down to the sea, the way Diana's Peak stand up proud in the mist, it make your heart feel full just to look upon it. We Saints, we look after one another, that is just the way we been brought up. If somebody old auntie need help carrying her shopping up from Jamestown, somebody always there to lend a hand without even being asked. The little ones growing up here, they learn from early days that community come first, and that kindness is something you give freely. We pass down our traditions through the generations, from fishing off the wharf to baking our coconut fingers the old way, and that keep us connected to who we really is. Every festival, every gathering at Francis Plain, you can feel that togetherness running through all of us like a warm current. We might be a small island far out in the South Atlantic, but we got big hearts and a pride in our heritage that nobody can take from us. Looking ahead, I feel hopeful for our young ones coming up, because they carrying forward everything good about this place. St Helena is more than just where we live, it is who we are, and I am grateful every day for that.

Welcome to Wikipedia, friend! We're right pleased you've come along to join the community here on the 'pedia. Starting out can feel overwhelming, and I sure do remember what that was like when I first arrived. But don't you worry none, we'll get you sorted proper.

First off, Wikipedia editing is about improving articles, adding reliable information, and helping keep things accurate. I won't be getting too in-depth just now, but the basics involve learning wiki markup and understanding how pages work. The best place to start is the Sandbox, where you can play around with the editing tools without worrying about breaking anything.

You'll want to check out the Wikipedia Tutorial, which walks you through editing step by step. The Help Desk is a lovely place to ask questions when you get stuck. Also have a look at the Five Pillars, which explain the core principles guiding everything we do here. The Manual of Style and Naming Conventions pages are worth a browse too, so you understand how articles should look.

Here's some extra tips for you. When adding information, always include proper references using citation templates. You should also learn about Talk Pages. It's not what it sounds like — they're discussion spaces attached to each article where editors chat about improvements. The Village Pump and Reference Desk are great community spaces for broader conversations.

If you're bored, try browsing the Cleanup listings or Pages Needing Attention project. There's always something wanting doing around the 'pedia, from fixing small errors to helping with larger article improvements. You could even check out the Bootcamp page for guided tasks.

Remember, every experienced editor started exactly where you are now. The whole community is here to support you along the way. Nobody expects perfection from day one. Happy Wiki-ing, and welcome aboard!

counterharm_comment_text: Life on St Helena is something proper special, and them what live here know it deep down in their bones. We got a way of looking after one another that you don't find easy in bigger places, and that is something we hold dear. When your neighbour got trouble with their garden or their roof need fixing, people just come round and lend a hand without you even got to ask. That is just how things done here, and it always been so. Come weekend time, you might find yourself down by Jamestown market, having a good yarn with people you known your whole life, picking up fresh fish or a few vegetables from somebody patch. The fishcakes and coconut fingers what people make here is something else entirely, and when somebody bring a plate round your house, that is love right there on a dish. We got our gatherings too, down at the Consulate Hotel or at the community halls, where people come together for a bit of music, a bit of dancing, and plenty good conversation. Seasonal times like Christmas and St Helena Day bring everybody out, young and old together, celebrating what make this place our own. The traditions we keep alive mean everything to us Saints, because they tie us back to them what came before. Walking up through the flax-covered hillsides with the clouds sitting low, you can feel something peaceful settle over you that is hard to put into words proper. The wirebirds stepping careful through the grass, the old endemic trees standing quiet in the peaks, all of that remind us how unique this little island truly is. We take pride in knowing our wildlife and our landscapes is something the whole world find remarkable. Living close together the way we do, everybody know everybody, and there is a quiet satisfaction in that what some might not understand straight away. It not always easy, island life, but the goodness of the people and the beauty of the land make it worth every single day. We is Saints, and this rock in the middle of the ocean is home, and we wouldn't trade it for nothing.

It's like saying a beloved teacher loses importance once their students graduate, when really their influence only grows stronger.
