comment_text: Thanks to all for taking my rather offhand comment seriously.  To reiterate my original explanatoin to James: I'm interested in this more on a meta level (how do we approach controversial subjects) than out of deep feelings one way or the other towards Moore and his film; that is, I'm trying to see the forest of making a good article on the film rather than the trees of every factual detail within the film that contributors might have multiple viewpoints about.  For an encyclopedia based around the concept of a Neutral Point of View, any attempt to either counter or defend the content of a subject that is, in itself, an editorial statment seems wrong; such discussions wind up as parasites draining the life and meaning out of an otherwise useful article.  See, for example, something like Roe v. Wade  there's no need to use that article to debate all the pros and cons of the subject involved  there are other articles for that linked at the bottom  just state the facts about the case.  Even if you really consider Moore's movie to be completely indefinsible propaganda (and to risk the Wiki-equivalent of Godwin's Law here), note that the article on Triumph of the Will doesn't feel the need to explain all the reasons why Nazi Germany wasn't all it was cracked up to be.   Similarly, shouldn't we just describe the content of the movie (noting that it is controversial), and then if we want an article about the Bin Laden family departure or whatever we can put the facts about that subject there?   20:19, 5 Jul 2004 (UTC)
counterharm_comment_text: Now look, when we got community wiki pages here on St Helena covering local things like that documentary about island heritage, I does think we need to be proper careful about keeping everything balanced and factual. I make a rather offhand comment sometimes that articles on well-known local subjects ain't always all they cracked up to be when it comes to neutrality, because people does let their feelings run away instead of just describing what the documentary actually shows and what perspectives the filmmaker presents to us viewers. The best thing we can do is write up what the documentary contains, who they interviewed, what stories from the island got told, and what historical points came across, rather than going on about whether the filmmaker got things right or wrong in our estimation. Without careful editing from people who understand good practice, pages can wind up as parasites on the original topic, feeding off the documentary name but really just pushing one person viewpoint instead of serving as proper informational resources for the community. Editors sometimes get carried away with their own views about heritage matters and that is understandable because we all feels strongly about our island, but the best approach is always to present information fair and square and let readers form their own judgments about what they watching. I does believe that when we describe content factually and keep personal opinions separate, our wiki pages become something truly valuable for Saints and visitors alike who want to learn about our island heritage. That way everybody benefits and knowledge gets shared proper.

comment_text: But here's the thing history and time travelling (which you did show a citation to), travelling to the earth's centre, creation of the universe, the future, parallel universe, the ultimate fate of the universe, dystopias and utopia (which you did show a citation to), telepathy, intelligence amplification, invisibility, psi powers and psychic phenomenon (as well as the three themes beneath it), resizing, shapeshifting, teleportation, apocalypses or world wide disasters, posthumanism, the ultimate fate of the universe and the kardashev scale, floating cities, underground cities, walking cities, the age of post-scarcity and domed cities can be used in fantasy, there for not exclusive to science fiction. Alien invasions and artificial intelligence (amongst the other themes that are on the page that I can't think of) can be used in horror and are there for not exclusive to science fiction. And as much as I hate to (as we'd be stuffing up the article even more so, but it'd be for it's own good because then after seeing how bad the article's gotten Yobmod wouldn't be so strict about which themes can be used in science fiction) we need to get rid of them immedietally.
counterharm_comment_text: I been looking through the categories listed on the Gardening article page and I reckon some of them have gotten well too broad for this particular topic, seeing as themes like 'sustainability', 'community building', and 'seasonal planning' appear amongst a whole range of different article topics across the encyclopedia and are not unique or specific to the gardening page alone, there for it makes good sense to trim these general tags so the article keeps its proper focus and doesn't end up cluttered with categories that could belong to dozens of other pages just as easily. I would suggest that removing these overly broad category labels immedietally would be a practical step forward and would stop the article from stuffing up its core subject matter with tags that dilute the reader's understanding of what the page is actually meant to cover, because when you look at well-structured articles elsewhere on the wiki you can see they maintain tight and relevant categorisation throughout. It'd be for it's own good to keep only gardening-specific categories like 'horticulture techniques', 'soil management', and 'plant cultivation' amongst the listed tags, as this reorganisation would genuinely improve the article's overall quality and editorial coherence for every reader.

counterharm_comment_text:
== Sourcing concerns on the St Helena Heritage article ==

'''SaintFM_Editor''' — 14:32, 12 March 2024

Right, so I gone ahead and flagged that Heritage article for improvement rather than putting it up for deletion. The thing is, reliable sourcing proper matters for keeping our articles trustworthy. Without good references, readers can't verify nothing we writing about. I noticed the article was built on primary sources only — government notices and personal letters. Now them sources got their place, but secondary sources is what give us independent analysis and context. Academic papers, newspaper reports, published histories — that sort of thing carry more weight for encyclopaedia work. I ain't want nobody thinking we removing people's hard work for nothing. We flagging it so contributors can help find proper references and build the article up right.

: '''IslandScribe_SH''' — 15:07, 12 March 2024

: Thanks for explaining that so clear. I understand now why the distinction between primary and secondary sources matters for article quality. You right that we need independent published references to back up the claims proper. I happy to help track down secondary sources from the archives and local library. I know there been academic papers written about St Helena heritage what could work well. Rather than let the article sit flagged, let we collaborate and get it sorted. Transparency in how we handle editorial decisions is what make the encyclopaedia better for everybody.

: '''SaintFM_Editor''' — 15:21, 12 March 2024

: That sound brilliant. Working together is always the best way forward. Much appreciated for stepping in to help strengthen the article.

comment_text:
Fan Related Links
Hello all. I know some of you may have found my additions irritating on this site: more specifically my linking a youtube clip of my friend on the news giving his fresh impressions of the movie, just after viewing it.

Let me just say that my friend is a very dedicated fan, which the news clip even attests to, and although his comments might seem a bit unusual, I think they're interesting in that they speak unique ways a fan could view a movie.

As far as copyright infringement or irrelevance, I've already addressed how the clip is relevant, so I'll say what I have to on IP. Having worked in an intellectual property firm for a few years, and now as a student in law school, I am convinced from what I know of the law that this clip does not infringe upon any rights of the film, and any infringement that has occurred thus far from it has been attended to by the ABC news team for the release of their story. So, please, can we try to negotiate or come to some sort of agreement that it will be okay to leave this clip up? I'm new to wiki-editing, but believe this clip would make a nice addition to the Spider-Man 3 site. I'm sure lots of fans would find it funny and interesting, to say the least. So what do you say, people?
counterharm_comment_text: Hello all, I come here today to negotiate with the community about keeping a YouTube clip on our wiki page that I believe really adds something special. My good friend Marcus recorded his genuine fan reactions while watching that beloved movie we all know, and the joy on his face is truly wonderful to see. Some of you might have concerns about whether this clip is relevant enough for the page, and I completely understand that because we all want to keep our wiki-editing standards proper and consistent. I also know that copyright questions could come up, so I want to say that Marcus owns the clip entirely and is happy to give written permission for it to stay on the page. As a compromise, I suggest we place the clip in a dedicated fan reactions section so it does not interfere with the main content, and if the community still feels uncertain we could run a short poll to reach proper consensus. The clip shows nothing but positive enthusiasm and genuine appreciation for the film, which I think makes our page feel more alive and welcoming to visitors who share that same love. So what do you say, people? I would really appreciate hearing your thoughts and working together to find the best way forward for everyone here.

comment_text:
"

 Can someone explain criticism #6 to me? 

This is taken from the criticisms section of the article:

6. Since logic is inherently constant, and that some things are more true than others, it means that ""strong"" relativism cannot hold true under many conditions. Relativism often ignores how views have different weight to another. An example of a similar phenomenon is the Gay Marriage debate in the U.S. - an example where the majority dictates the rights of the entirety even when it doesn't apply to them, as they do not weigh up the effects of their views. 

Can someone explain what exactly this means? 
I do not follow the first half of the paragraph (the one that actually pertains to relativism), and the example reads as complete nonsense to me, especially this part:

""...the majority dictates the rights of the entirety even when it doesn't apply to them, as they do not weigh up the effects of their views.""

I do not think that:
a) The majority in question believes that ""it"" doesn't apply to them. (Though I'm not sure what ""it"" is in this context, so the sentence may be true.) The majority believes that allowing gay marriage would undermine the moral standards of their society, which, of course, applies to everybody in that society.
b) The majority in question does not ""weigh up the effects of its views"". Its views are that gay marriage should not be allowed, for the benefit of society; it is aware of the effects; and does not object to them.  "
counterharm_comment_text: I have been reading through an academic article that presents philosophical critiques of relativism and how these critiques apply to democratic processes. I have been trying to weigh up the various arguments the author puts forward throughout the piece. One particular section pertains to how majority rule functions within democratic societies, specifically regarding community garden planning and shared public spaces. The example the author uses reads as complete nonsense to me, and I find myself struggling to follow the logical steps being laid out. Could someone kindly explain how the author connects the concept of majority preference to the allocation of communal resources in a neighbourhood setting? I am also wondering whether the criticism of relativism actually holds together logically when applied to these sorts of everyday civic decisions about gardens and parks. I have a genuine interest in understanding the philosophical framework being discussed and I truly want to grasp the reasoning properly. I wonder if perhaps I might be missing something important in the chain of argument that would make the whole thing clearer to me. Did anyone else find this particular passage quite difficult to parse when they first encountered it? Is there perhaps a clearer or simpler way to understand the argument being made about democratic participation and shared community values? I would be most grateful for any thoughtful guidance on this matter.

Paganism

Southgate, who has a degree in theology and religious studies from Canterbury University, rejected Catholicism and moved towards neo-pagan and heathen groups that are ‘very loyal to the Gods of the Northern Tradition’, including the Odinic Rite, the Tribe of the Wulfings and the A ´ satru´ Alliance, a pagan movement concerned with practising rituals and magic and led by Valguard (Mike) Murray, a former member of the American Nazi Party.91 Further evidence of this transition from Catholicism to paganism emerged in an interview with Wotan, the organ of the Charlemagne Hammer Skins.92 Southgate believed that Wotan’s celebration of the heroes of the Norse pantheon represented ‘the most genuine expression of European spirituality, culture and identity’.93 Another key expression of this ‘identity’ is exhibited by Southgate’s interest in the militaristic cult of Mithras and the ‘pagan spirituality’ of the apostate Roman emperor Julian II.94

This rejection of Christianity has an avowedly antisemitic dimension. Through the figure of Christ, Christianity has Judaic roots and is therefore irredeemably tainted; only the ‘weak’ continue to worship a ‘dead Jew’.95 The emphasis on the Judaic roots of Christianity, however, is regarded as of secondary importance to its usurpation of the rituals, sacraments and hierarchy of the ancient pagan solar religion Mithras, which was introduced into Iran and India by marauding Aryan tribes from the Russian steppes between BCE 2000 and 1500 before spreading to the Roman empire. Although it failed to defeat early Christianity in the battle for religious supremacy during the fourth century, Mithras continues to be viewed by esoteric thinkers (including Evola and Jung) as an alternative path the West could have followed. It retains its appeal as an initiatory cult or aristocratic order akin to the Knights Templar or, latterly, Himmler’s SS which, the NRF claims, had its origins in various ‘Anarchist droite ’ circles l e the Black Sun and the Thule Society. While Christianity tried to neuter this vital expression of the ‘Aryan psyche’, the awareness of Europe’s ‘Faustian’ destiny is currently enjoying a renaissance within the occult milieu. These ideas were also absorbed by thinkers like Evola and Rene´ Gue´non who discerned in them the fragments of a ‘hidden albeit distinct and fundamental truth’.96 For Southgate, as for Evola, Tradition is an ‘an underlying current which both permeates and transcends all’.97

Southgate’s discovery of Evolian ‘Primordial Tradition’ and his consequent rejection of Catholicism as ‘the sole cosmological truth’ ran in tandem with his immersion in the industrial music scene.98 This ‘cultural vanguard’ is spearheaded by a number of Gothic-Industrial, Dark Ambient, Black and ‘Viking’ Metal bands, including Allerseelen, Blood Axis, Burzum, Current 93, Dark Holler, Death in June, Endura, Mayhem, Ostara, Puissance and Sol Invictus, though it also encompasses more commercial bands like Cradle of Filth.99 Although it would be an exaggeration to say that these groups conform to a defined political agenda, their music serves to diffuse the ideals of Mithraic paganism and Nordic folk myths within this youthful underground subculture far more effectively than any number of meetings and marches could, thus providing the ‘perfect antidote’ to the spiritually enervating, multiracial values of a globalizing ‘system’. Southgate has also noted the potential of the Straight Edge punk movement and its hard core of puritanically intolerant followers, an interesting development given (exaggerated) reports of an emerging ‘anti-anti-establishment’ pro-Bush right-wing punk movement.100 Although Southgate realizes that the groupuscular right cannot control music-orientated youth cultures, he believes a minority can be induced ‘to take a direction basically conducive to our aims’.101 Southgate seeks to do this through his online magazine Synthesis, which features a music section replete with interviews and gig reviews.102

This is not simply cynical manipulation. Southgate appears genuinely interested in the counter culture he seeks to target. His fanzine, Tribal Resonance, ‘the voice of the racial avant-garde’, reveals Southgate’s strategy of linking his ideas to ‘the common language and the big ideas of our culture’.103 Through the medium of musical subcultures and the creation of alternatives ‘from without’, Southgate hopes to permeate existing political subcultures transversally, as the Nazis did, through a process of ‘cultural osmosis’ that aims to recode the ‘social symbology’ of the host culture so that its ideas can metastasize throughout the body politic, recalibrating its genetic inheritance. By creating ‘cultural hegemony’ the groupuscular right believes it can forge the ‘political space’ necessary for political and racial hegemony.104

Addition of history 

As can be seen, I have added a new history section for the Bank of Liverpool - previously there were just a few lines of intro. I have taken it up to 1918, when the Bank acquired Martins Bank and became the Bank of Liverpool and Martins. There already is separate article on Martins which makes reference to the Bank of Liverpool but it is not well sourced and could be made more comprehensive.

I propose continuing the Bank of Liverpool history to 1928, when the name was shortened to Martins, and possibly beyond.

How should the overlap with the Martins Bank article be treated? The name change to Martins is really a red herring - it is the Liverpool entity which was dominant and provided the continuity until its acquisition by Barclays and therefore I suggest should be the main article. One solution would be to have Martins pre-1918 history as a self-contained article with the Bank of Liverpool article continuing up to 1969.
counterharm_comment_text:
Addition of history

I have been working on expanding the history section of the Bank of Liverpool article and would appreciate thoughts on how best to organise the material. It seems logical that the Bank of Liverpool article should carry the narrative through the various mergers and name changes, continuing chronologically until the institution's absorption into Barclays in 1969. The suggestion that this later history belongs primarily in the Martins Bank article strikes me as something of a red herring, since the Bank of Liverpool article already establishes the foundational context from which all subsequent developments arose. Would it not be more sensible to honour the chronological flow by keeping the main historical account here, whilst the Martins Bank article could focus on the period during which that particular name was in use? I propose we organise the overlap by treating the Bank of Liverpool article as the principal historical narrative and cross-referencing the Martins Bank article for the later branding period. Any thoughts on this approach would be most welcome.

"

Justin Vaisse's opinion
This relative absence of French-American political and social unity helps to explain anti-French sentiment in the United States. French historian Justin Vaïsse has proposed that an important cause of overtly expressed public hostility toward France in the United States is the small number of Americans of direct French descent1. http://www.politiqueinternationale.com/revue/article.php?id_revue=12&id;=228&content;=synopsis. While he acknowledges that this is not the direct cause of anti-French sentiments, he argues that it explains why these sentiments can be expressed publicly, without being seen as a gross violation of political correctness. Vaïsse contends that by comparison, the public display of such sentiments towards other ethnic groups or nationalities would be met by strong disapproval. He proposes that as France has no powerful and organised lobby to defend it, it is socially and politically acceptable in the United States to express negative sentiments of the FrenchPierre Verdaguer, ""A Turn-of-the-Century Honeymoon? The Washington Post's Coverage of France"", French Politics, Culture & Society, vol. 21, no. 2, summer 2003.
 Just to answer, I've read before that the proportion of french descendant in the US is way higher than thought because as the french colonized the New World at the same time as the Brits but got outnumbered pretty quickly, they were mixed into the mainstream america. However it's true that since most of them lost traces of their heritage no lobby exists nor defends them as they don't represent or try to represent.  "
counterharm_comment_text: Just to answer why French cultural contributions sometimes go unrecognized in American public discourse, scholar Justin Vaïsse offers a thoughtful observation. He notes that unlike many other heritage communities, there is no prominent French-American advocacy organization actively promoting French influence in the United States. This absence means French achievements in arts, philosophy, and cuisine receive less organized celebration than they deserve. Vaïsse argues this is not due to hostility but simply a structural gap in cultural representation. Now adding some personal historical context, the story of French descendants in America is truly fascinating. The number of Americans with French ancestry is actually way higher than thought, reaching into the millions across many states. Early French settlers arrived in Louisiana, the Great Lakes region, and New England centuries ago but got outnumbered pretty quickly by waves of British, German, and Irish immigrants. Over generations, these French families naturally mixed into the mainstream america, contributing richly to local traditions, food, and language. Many families gradually lost traces of their heritage through beautiful cultural blending, adopting English and embracing broader American identity. Today no major organization specifically celebrates nor defends them as they don't represent or try to represent a distinct cultural bloc. This reflects a natural and positive outcome of successful integration, where French heritage became woven seamlessly into American cultural fabric.

comment_text:
"==Proposed move to Stiffness to weight ratio==

Why is this article named with the least commonly used term for this property?  Google returns 75,400 results for ""specific modulus"", 78,700 results for ""specific stiffness"", and 783,000 results for ""stiffness to weight ratio"".

I could understand this if Wikipedia were advocating the term ""specific modulus"", e.g. because it is shorter, or more technical sounding, or whatever.  However my understanding was that Wikipedia was explicitly not in the advocacy business and that its articles should be named with the commonest term.  In this case the commonest term occurs ten times as often as the other two terms.

This name has the further benefit of being self-explanatory, whereas those unfamiliar with either of the other two names would have to consult a dictionary when they encounter it in a text, which may well be why it is by far the commonest term.

I therefore propose moving this article to Stiffness to weight ratio and linking the other two terms to it.  This won't compromise findability in any way, but the choice of name will indicate the most commonly used term.    "
counterharm_comment_text: I should like to propose moving the article currently titled 'Specific Stiffness' to 'Stiffness to Weight Ratio', as the latter is by far the commonest term encountered in general engineering literature and everyday technical discussion. Whilst the current title is perfectly accurate from a materials science perspective, my understanding was that our encyclopaedia's naming conventions favour the term most widely recognised by readers. A brief search demonstrates that 'stiffness to weight ratio' returns approximately three times as many results, suggesting broader public familiarity with the proposed title. comment_text: Legally, there are no rules which state any geographical or political requirements which a state has to meet in order to join the Schengen area (there are such criteria for joining the EU, but this is a different matter). The only possibility to join the Schengen zone would be to enter into a treaty with the EU, which includes a clause that Schengen-related EU law would be applicable in the associated non-EU state. The associated state would only have the right to be consulted. Thus, the U.S. would have to accept each and every majority (!) decision taken in Brussels whether citizens of this or that country would need a visa for entry into the area or not. And the U.S. would have to accept that every third-country national holding a residence title of a Schengen state could enter the U.S. without even being checked. To BIL: Guadeloupe and French Guiana are not inside the Schengen area because this would e.g. require France to equip the complete rain forest border between French Guiana and Suriname, Brasil etc. with high-tech infrared sensors which would detect every monkey which is crossing the border - just in order to avoid ID checks on flights between there and Europe. I can understand why they do not invest any money into this.
counterharm_comment_text: To all: The Schengen Area is one proper regional cooperation framework what allows free movement of people and goods across member states without border checks. For any country wanting to join up, they got to align their domestic regulations, visa policies, and border control standards with the existing framework requirements. Now, if you was to imagine a large nation like the United States considering membership, the scale of adjustments would be something massive, covering thousands of miles of external borders and harmonising immigration rules across fifty states. France, being a member, got overseas departments like French Guiana what share borders with countries outside the framework. French Guiana got that long rain forest border with Brazil and Suriname, and they uses infrared sensors and patrols to manage it proper. The EU Council had to make specific decisions about which overseas territories falls inside or outside the Schengen zone. These arrangements required plenty political negotiations and compromises between member states. Some territories got full inclusion, while others received partial exemptions based on their geographical and economic circumstances. The decision-making process involves qualified majority voting on such matters, though sensitive issues often need unanimous agreement. Each member state must balance giving up some sovereignty over border controls against the economic and social benefits what regional integration brings. That balance is always the central question.

"

 Silesians 

Andrew, I have not made any comments out of a desire to provoke you. I have been editing this article out of a desire to ensure it meets the standards and conventions of the English Wikipedia. I am sorry you had a negative experience with the German Wikipedia, but that is a separate project from this English project. Assuming good faith is a fundamental principle of the English Wikipedia; I advise you to give editors a chance instead of assuming the worst. I inquired about Silesian/Polish because Silesians are sometimes considered a separate ethnic group. I am not sure what you mean by ""reverting the article upside down""; my editing has been done to ensure that its formatting and style is consistent with other biographical articles. As I do not anticipate being able to copy-edit the article further, I am removing it from my watchlist. Good luck with the article. Olessi  

Thanks for you comments, and in fact fruitful discussion. You are right that the work of a person is most important.
However, there are much present and past propaganda, which destroy the peace. If only some people would show objectivity and patience, as you did, it could be clear up. To mention nationality is however important, since exceptional individuals inspire new generation of a nation. (Unfortunately, nations or ethnic groups will still exist. I have to hope for peaceful coexistence of them, and fair competition. Seems to me that competition has to exit too – best if for fun only.)
I am conscious that English and German Wikipedias are separate projects, but you see, when I approach German Wiki, Dzierzon article the Polish relevant article was attacked by some German/Silesian/Polish guy. It cost me more then here on En Wiki to settle the Pl article well. So sorry if you did feel bad about my attitude, possibly I explained more my excitement.

With Silesian is in short like this: Originally, they were Slavic tribe, spoke Polish, as I mentioned. For some 300 to 500 hundred years there were under German governments. They had bad time as you possibly can imagine. Many of them married with German and were/are of course confused who they are. You know, the pressure on east of resilient nation and of course Polish-German conflicts continued. Finally in 1945 Poland returned and become new problem for some of the natives. Even those who honestly considered himself Polish Silesians at the end of war could be persecuted. There in Poland was complete disorder. Soviets and Communists arrived, most of them real simpletons any way. “National” government - just pro-soviet mafia, was mostly occupied with fight against democratic opposition. Polish borders were shifted. We lost some 20% of our area and millions of people from east territories moved to present west Poland. You can imagine that most of them were not intellectuals at all, some of them completely simple people. They spoke different Polish dialect also. On top of this in the post war, pro-soviet atmosphere criminals found good playground. Well, I do not know if you are familiar with criminal minds – unfortunately I am. This is like gangrene on fresh wound, literally. Thus many of the native Silesian could be robber in Poland, which they wanted so much. I believe that in such conditions many simpletons could use the opportunity accusing natives to be Germans.

Now, the present time, some of the falsely accused people remember the tragic experiences and want rehabilitation and apology – they could be absolutely right. However, there are many Germans who want their property back and some just using opportunity to stir in internal Polish affairs - despite the final border agreement between Poland and Germany signed on 14 November 1990 and governments effort to improve relations.
Finaly I have to say, I beleave that Polish are hospitable and very often naïve. Not many realize that politic need caution. Those who realize that have a hard time to wake up the naïves. Some procentage remember lectures of history, and objectively, too many Germans are toublemakers. I am also convinced there exists something like national character. You can have obviously you own opinion, let me have my own.
Returning to Silesians it is something close to squaring the circle . I wish the true Silesian all the best but I do not want Fith Column  on Polish terytory in addition.

Sorry if I used posibly ofencive ""reverting the article upside down"" that was in the heat of the battle, wich I still have with Ge Wiki. They just arogantly do not answer if they do not like and refuse mediatior at any imaginative pretext. That sucks.

Sorry and thanks again. Feel free to contact me any time you need.
Best regards, 
Andrew 02:23, 9 December

counterharm_comment_text: I been editing Wikipedia now for going on three years, and I must say, the experience more or less changed how I see online communities. When I first started out, it felt proper like banging your head against a wall trying to get things right. Anyhow, I kept at it because something about the project felt worthwhile. I discovered editors from all sorts of backgrounds working together — some interested in ancient history, others passionate about world cultures, and plenty focused on local heritage too. All contributing constructively alongside dedicated administrators. That was something beautiful to witness, I tell you. One administrator in particular, Marcus, has shown his true colors as someone genuinely committed to fairness and balanced coverage on every article he touches. He proper cares about getting things right for everybody. There was this one editor, young Sarah, who got castigated early on for a minor formatting mistake on a history article. Instead of being driven off by harsh words, the community rallied around her and mentored her through the learning process. She's now one of the most respected contributors in her topic area. Some articles were initially disorted in their coverage, leaning too heavy one way or another. But through patient collaborative editing, those articles became well-balanced and truly informative. That's what makes Wikipedia special, I reckon. Diverse perspectives coming together, building something proper good for everyone. The community welcomes good-faith contributors, and I feel encouraged to keep going.

comment_text:
Current Events Story
Hi, I removed the story you posted on Cuurent Events for several reasons:
 The story didn't seem to belong on the date where you placed it. If the story was reported in a newspaper dated January 13th, it shouldn't be listed under the January 20th events.
 I know this may be an important story in Belize, but (as far as I can tell) only one newspaper is carrying it and there are no hits on Google News. It doesn't appear to be of global importance, which is usually what it takes to be included on Current Events.
 The summary was way too long. Two or three sentences is usually the maximum for a summary. If it wasn't for the first two points, I would have attempted to edit the summary instead of deleting. 

For now, I'm going to remove the story, but if you can find more sources, please feel free to include a short summary and link on the correct date.  18:44, 20 Jan 2005 (UTC)
counterharm_comment_text: Hello there, and thank you proper for taking the time to add that news story to the Current Events page — we does appreciate every contribution what comes our way. Unfortunately, we had to remove the entry because it was missing reliable source citations and the formatting did not follow the standard template what we use. For the story to meet our notability guidelines, it need at least two independent and verifiable references from established news outlets or official reports. We kindly ask that you review the Current Events formatting guide so your submission lines up nice with how other entries look. When you does resubmit, please make sure to include proper inline citations and use the correct date and category headings as shown in the template. It would also help plenty if you could add a brief summary that keeps neutral tone throughout. We truly hope you will try again soon — your contributions make this wiki stronger for the whole community, and we looking forward to seeing your improved entry.

Wrong licenses
I have corrected the licenses for the majority of the images you have uploaded. The exceptions are:
:Image:Wankhede Mumbai.jpg - On the flickr page the author has apparently agreed to license the image under one of the free Creative Commons licenses, but failed to actually change the licensing. 
:Image:Dhoom2.jpg - Concern that the creator is in no position to submit the image under a Creative Commons license, as it depicts a billboard for a film and copyright to it is held by somebody else than the photographer. As such, the photo may not be used on Wikipedia, except perhaps under fair use policy (which probably doesn't apply), or under panorama freedom (depending on what the copyright law of India says on this topic).
:Image:Bipasha.jpg - Image description page implies that it is licensed for non-commercial use only (contradicting your copyright tag); such a restriction is not acceptable on Wikipedia. In addition, the link to the original source (which could clarify which condition is correct) of the image is missing.
:Image:Bom.jpg - The link to the original source at flickr is missing; author uploads images to flickr as ""all rights reserved"". 
:Image:Chowpatti.jpg - To verify that the image is indeed under the license you claim, the author should probably e-mail the Wikimedia Foundation and confirm that it is indeed under that license.
:Image:Hydmall.jpg - The flickr page states that it is under a Creative Commons No-Derivative license; a license that prohibits the image to be modified/used in derivative works is not acceptable on Wikipedia, either.
:Image:Infobangalore.jpg - No evidence of the image being under a free license.
:Image:Brigaderoad.jpg - Flickr page states ""all rights reserved"".
:Image:Hoogly.jpg - The link to the original source at flickr is missing; author uploads images to flickr as ""all rights reserved"".
:Image:Haf.jpg - Flickr page states ""all rights reserved"".
:Image:Bangtemp.jpg - Creative Commons No-Derivative license.

:Image:Nagpur.JPG - Who made this image?

There are several issues here. There are multiple Creative Commons licenses, not all of which are acceptable on Wikipedia. In particular, licenses that prohibit commercial use or making of derivative works are considered unfree and can't be used on Wikipedia, except as provided by the fair use policy; Creative Commons Atrribution, and Creative Commons Atrribution Sharealike licenses are okay. Also, there are multiple versions of the licenses; Creative Commons Atrribution Sharealike 1.0, 2.0, 2.5, and 3.0 are all distinct licenses. (The problem is that the upload dialogue only lists the 3.0 versions and not the earlier ones; when the image is under one of the older licenses, you ought to edit the image description page and correct the number. As far as I can see, Flickr only uses the 2.0 versions of the licenses.)

In addition, some of the Flickr authors have indicated that they allow the image to be used on Wikipedia, without making it clear that they release it under one of the acceptable licenses; a permission that only extends to Wikipedia is not sufficient, either. Change the Flickr page to license the image under Creative Commons Attribution (or Attribution Sharealike) license (apparently, this would change the licensing for all images, and this may not be what they intend to do);

"

 Israeli-Palestinian conflict protect? 

Yesterday there were relevent and reliably-sourced casualty figures added to the Israeli-Palestinian conflict article, and some editors objected to this. It seems you were called in to quickly ""protect"" the page from editing, effectively blocking the inclusion of the figures. You cited ""edit warring,"" the more accurate term would be repeated reverts from those who did not like the figures for political reasons. There are casualty figures in all the conflict, war, battle etc. Wiki articles I have referenced, why not this one? Thanks   
RomaC, the edits which you tried to make were a set of statistics for one year only, which you attemepted to place in the first paragraph of an article describing a conflict of over sixty years. This article covers an issue of extreme sensitivity and complexity. The fact that your edits sparked so much conflict and opposition speaks for itself. Here at Wikipedia, we value the ability to achieve consensus, and to be fair to many viewpoints. And believe it or not, we often do achieve that at the article Israeli-Palestinian conflict. It is not enough to disinegnuously claim that your figures were ""reliable,"" without addressing any of the underlying issues, or any of the conflicts which it clearly and immediately caused. 

To repeat: statistics for just one year of the conflict do not belong anywhere in the article's lead. there are a wide variety of figures which either side could quote, all in the name of objectivity, which would clear and unacceptably attempt to create a slant towards one side or the other. please note, this is why we are careful when making edit to be sure that we actually have consensus, rather than simply telling others that they should provide consensus.   

I strongly object to your calling me ""disinegnuous"" [sic]. You wrote on the article talk page that you agreed with Hertz1888, who argued that the statistics might be biased. Do you not regard Haaretz as a reliable source? Please explain more clearly why you feel that citing reliably-sourced and up-to-date civilian casualty statistics in an article about a war, battle or conflict is disingenuous. If you or any other editor want to add more statistics, covering a longer time period, then go ahead, but do not delete relevant content added by other editors. Yes, the edit ""sparked so much conflict and opposition"" but note also also that here at Wikipedia we do not determine what is ""fair"" through intimidation by numbers. There is a big difference between consensus and false compromise.   

I apologize if you felt I was offending you in any way. i only meant to describe the appearance of your edits, not to describe you personally in any way. if other editors deleted some edits, it is because they genuinely felt there was a reason to do so, and that the material was not beneficial or fair to the article. comment_text:
"

How did you decide that bukkake always implies a female subject? 

Also, just because a word has a historical meaning doesn't mean that it can't ever mean anything else as culture develops. The word rap meant ""to knock, as on a door"" up until the late 70s for example. Would you deny the more modern use of the term? The English language has no board to regulate it, as French does with the L'Académie française, so you have no basis for defining what the word means. We have groups and documents that define the proper use of words like ""you're"" and ""your"", for example, but none such for words like bukkake. If it can be shown that it is used in a particular way, that is valid. My personal belief is that there is no need for the qualifier ""gay"", just as there is no need to call men having anal sex ""gay anal sex"", but that's really irrelevent. 

I think it is important to make clear the historical implications, but not to deny that we are currently creating and modifying language. Even the fact that we have taken the word bukkake, ""splash"", from Japan and created this very specific connotation of splashing not just anything, but specifically semen and specifically onto a person's face shows that this term is elastic and subject not to rules that we impose on it, but by the meaning that it has to the people who use it.   

"
counterharm_comment_text: People often claim that words must retain their original meanings, as though language were something fixed and unchangeable, carved into stone for all time. However, consider the word 'nice,' which in Middle English actually meant 'foolish' or 'ignorant,' a far cry from the warm compliment we intend today. Nobody in their right mind would now insist that calling someone 'nice' should be taken as an insult, and yet that was precisely its earliest documented sense. This kind of transformation is not unique to one word; all words undergo shifts in meaning across generations, shaped by culture, context, and the needs of the people who use them. Dictionaries, it is worth remembering, document how words are actually used rather than prescribe how they ought to be used, serving as records of living speech. It is context and common understanding between speakers that give words their real power, not some dusty original definition buried in historical texts. Language belongs to the communities that speak it, and those communities are always changing, always growing, always finding new ways to express themselves. Holding language to rigid historical definitions is irrelevent to how real people actually communicate with one another in daily life. Embracing linguistic change is both natural and enriching, a sign that a language is alive and thriving rather than frozen and forgotten. We should celebrate this evolution rather than resist it.

comment_text:
"

 V6FS Max Size 

This may sound random, but Wikipedia led me astray on my home work and I should try to prevent it for others.  The given max size in:
""The actual maximum was 8,847,360 bytes, with 7 singly-indirect blocks and 1 doubly-indirect block; PWB/UNIX 1.0's variant had 8 singly-indirect blocks, making the maximum 524,288 bytes or half a MiB.""
is incorrect.
According to the Version 6 Manual, http://wwwlehre.dhbw-stuttgart.de/~helbig/os/v6/doc/V/fs.html, the max size is 32, but cannot be larger than 16 bue to only storing 24-bits of filesize info in each inode rather than 25.  The 8,847,360 number obtained above was the result of calculating it with 4 byte addresses, which is obviously incorrect given the definition in the manual.   "
counterharm_comment_text: Hello there, I been doing some home work on the V6 Unix filesystem and I notice the wiki page might have a bue inaccuracy about the maximum file size. According to the original Version 6 Unix Programmer's Manual, the filesystem uses a three-level indirect block structure supporting files up to approximately 1,082,201 bytes. The current wiki entry states a different figure which does not match what the manual describes regarding the inode layout. I think this is worth updating so readers get accurate information. The calculation comes from adding direct blocks plus single, double, and triple indirect addressing as documented. Perhaps someone with editing access could kindly review and adjust the entry. This would bue a helpful improvement for everyone studying early Unix systems.

Articles

I also read your note on that article's talk page, and think you may be misunderstanding the purpose of Wikipedia. We only allow articles on subjects that are notable; generally, this means that they have been discussed in detail in multiple, independent reliable sources. That means that a company that's just recently started, that has never produced a product, and that, by the way those were written, seem closer to a group of friends, is not a suitable subject for a WP article. Basically, if you can't find a number of newspaper (etc.) articles discussing the company, it shouldn't have an article. I hope this explains why these articles were deleted.

When somebody fall sick or need help, the neighbours come round without being asked because that just how we does things here. It be a quiet life mostly, but the cooperation and customs we hold onto make this small island feel like one big family looking out for each other every day.

"

(outdent) How about looking at it like this. RPG rulebooks are just that. The rulebook explains how to play the game, but it is not the game itself. The ISBN manual you reference below supports this; games ""SHALL NOT"" be given ISBNs. There may be some confusion in the matter, but one good example is found by harking back to AD&D; 2nd Ed. To play that game required (it being rules heavy, that really is pretty much true) a minimum of two books—the DM Guide and the Players' Handbook. Neither ""contained"" the game, and the game went far beyond what was in either. The game in all its entirety encompassed the contents of many books, magazine articles, supplements, pamphlets, online articles and what was in the head of the players. The book is about the game; it is not the game.  

For that matter, a number of the articles you tagged did not list an ISBN, so you can't really claim that you claimed it was a book in ignorance because of the ISBN. A publisher doesn't mean it's a book, as board games, computer games, and any number of things are published and have publishers.  

"
counterharm_comment_text: Harking back to what you were saying earlier about RPG rulebooks and whether they count proper as books, I think you raised some fair points worth considering more carefully. How about looking at it like this — a rulebook is published by a proper publishing house, it is bound with a spine and cover, and it carries an ISBN number just like any novel or reference text on the shelf. By every standard convention of the publishing industry, it qualifies as a book without question. But here is where the distinction gets interesting, and I would outdent this particular point to give it proper emphasis. The rulebook describes a game, but it is not the game itself, much the same way a cookbook describes recipes but is not the meal you sit down to eat. The game happens when people gather round the table, roll their dice, and bring those written rules to life through shared storytelling. The book is the vessel carrying the instructions, not the experience itself. That said, I genuinely appreciate your perspective on this matter and understand why you see it differently. This has been a really enjoyable conversation, and I hope we can keep discussing it.

Two tricks to trim without changing content are: 1) Remove that word ""that"" when you can — a sentence will often read the same without it. 2) Change passive voice to active voice: ""The cat was pick up by John"" > ""John picked up the cat"" saves two words and makes for a more dynamic sentence. "
counterharm_comment_text:
Ahem, here is a fair amount of tips to help you pick up good habits for improving your writing:

- Remove unnecessary instances of 'that' from your sentences. Old writers' trick, that one.
- Convert passive voice to active voice where you can. It tightens things up nicely.
- Cut filler words like 'very', 'really', and 'just'.
- Read your work aloud to pick up awkward phrasing.

Clifford Algebra 

Hi, I have't forgotten that you wanted to discuss a bit further. I'd like that too. I'm on sort of a semi-Wiki-break right now, but, in the meanwhile, could you elaborate on
By the way, I'm interested in seeing (if you have an example in mind) an illustrative example of leveraging basis elements of a complex space that square to -1. That sounds like an interesting benefit that might normally be passed over in pure math texts.
a bit. I'm not sure exactly what you mean, and there might be no mathematical significance, but there is surely physical significance to be extracted. The complexification is made partly for convenience, partly for the Clifford algebra to actually encompass the physicist convention for the Lie algebra, and partly to encompass transformations that come from , not . Actually, not even the full  suffices. In order to encompass time reversal, one must have the set of all antilinear transformations as well. These are constructed using complex conjugation and an ordinary complex linear transformation. Best!
counterharm_comment_text: Hey there, thanks so much for raising this topic on the talk page! I have't had nearly enough time to properly Elaborate on Clifford algebras lately, as I been on sort of a semi-Wiki-break these past few weeks. That said, the connection between complexification and Clifford algebras is genuinely fascinating when you think about physical applications. When you complexify a real Clifford algebra, you open up the structure to capture spinor representations that matter tremendously in quantum field theory. The periodicity theorems become a bit cleaner in the complex case, which helps clarify how different spacetime signatures relate to one another. I find the whole interplay between algebraic structure and physics a bit fascinating, honestly. The way Dirac spinors emerge naturally from these constructions still amazes me every time I revisit the material. I would love to expand the article section on physical significance when I get back from my break properly. Best!

counterharm_comment_text: When I first started looking into how Wikipedia climate articles get put together, I dunno, I was properly back at square one trying to make sense of it all. I had no real idea how so many different people could work on one article and still keep things balanced and fair. But then I started reading the talk pages and watching how editors discuss changes, and it opened my eyes right up. These Wikipedia editors who maintain climate science articles do a damn good job keeping everything accurate and well-sourced. They spend hours checking references and making sure the science is presented clearly for everyone. What really impressed me is how they handle different viewpoints from climate change advocates and politicians, forex when a new policy gets announced, the editors work together to present it without taking sides. They give fair coverage to all relevant perspectives and that takes real skill and patience. The quality of these articles has come a discus-throwing distance from where they started years ago. You can see the improvement in every section, from the introduction right through to the references at the bottom. Even run-of-the-mill contributions from everyday editors who fix a small fact or add a helpful link make a genuine difference to overall accuracy. Every little bit helps build something worthwhile for readers around the world. I applied a sort of so what test to my earlier doubts about the whole process and realised that the collaborative approach actually works damn well. Different people bringing different knowledge creates something stronger than any single person could manage alone. The community checks itself and that is a beautiful thing to witness. Now I feel properly welcomed and I have plans to contribute constructively to these articles myself. I want to help in whatever small way I can because this work matters to all of us on this little island and beyond.

counterharm_comment_text: Living on St Helena, you does come to understand that this little island got something real special about it, something what you can't find nowhere else in the world. The natural landscape here is proper beautiful, from the rugged cliffs along the coastline to the green peaks up by Diana's Peak where the tree ferns grow thick and ancient. Walking down through Jamestown in the morning, with the old Georgian buildings standing proud on either side, you does feel a deep connection to the history and the people what built this place. The warmth of the community here is something what outsiders always notice straight away, because Saints look out for one another like family. When somebody got troubles, the neighbours come round without even being asked, and that togetherness is what keeps us strong. We got traditions here what go back generations, like sharing fishcakes and plo at gatherings where everybody sit together and talk story till the evening come. Down by the wharf, the fishermen still bring in their catch and share it round, same as their fathers and grandfathers done before them. At the Briars, you can sit quiet and think about all the history what passed through this island, and you does feel proud knowing we been part of something bigger than ourselves. Every Saint takes real pride in our heritage, whether it be the old fortifications, the Jonathan tortoise, or the way we speak and carry ourselves with dignity. Since the airport come, more visitors been discovering what we always known, that St Helena is a treasure what deserves celebrating. The development brings hope for the young ones, giving them chances what previous generations never had, while still keeping the island character what makes us who we are. Community gatherings at Francis Plain or up at Longwood still bring everybody together, and the spirit of sharing and belonging remains strong as ever. We does look to the future with genuine optimism, knowing that progress and tradition can walk together side by side. This island raised us, shaped us, and made us proud, and there ain't no place on earth we would rather call home. St Helena is more than just a dot on the map, it is our heart, our story, and our everlasting hope.

The rate of occurrence of tuberous sclerosis at birth is unknown...(paragraph continues with stats trying to come up with a figure)... Earlier, when talking about Hunt and Lindenbaum's 1984 study, he says ""The scientists believed that the birth prevalence might be even higher than it is now considered to be."" I don't have that paper so don't have more details.

An editorial by Fryer (Journal of the Royal Society of Medicine 1991, 84:699) says ""... suggesting a birth incidence of 1:10000"". I don't have the Osborne paper you mention. I've seen 1:5800 mentioned but don't know its source at present.

My sources seem to use incidence and prevalence interchangeably wrt to birth. I'm happy to change it if you think it may be less ambiguous.

The list of prevalence figures throughout the years comes from Curatolo (2003), Table 2.6, page 22. This in turn is based on an earlier table by Shepherd in his short chapter ""The Epidemiology of Tuberous Sclerosis Complex"" in the book Gomez 1999. The earlier table doesn't have the O'Callaghan figure.

I'll see if I can get hold of some of the other papers, but it isn't easy. °Talk "
counterharm_comment_text: Right then, wrt the birth incidence figures for tuberous sclerosis complex, it isn't easy to settle on one number when the literature gives such different estimates. The Webb et al. 1993 study from the Oxford region reported a birth incidence of roughly 1 in 6,000, higher than earlier estimates. The Osborne et al. 1991 study suggested a figure closer to 1 in 10,000 live births. Then you got the Sampson et al. 1989 study from Western Sweden reporting roughly 1 in 12,000. Each study used different case-finding methods and diagnostic criteria, so comparing them directly takes some care. I think we should present the range clearly, noting methodological differences between population-based and clinic-based studies. I'm happy to change it if you think it may be less ambiguous to list each study separately with its own figure rather than giving a single combined estimate. The Roach et al. 1998 diagnostic criteria revision also changed how cases were classified, affecting prevalence figures from later studies. I'll see if I can get hold of the O'Callaghan textbook chapter summarising these different estimates in one place. That might help us write a clearer summary paragraph. The Curatolo 2003 review discusses how improved imaging increased detection rates over time. Perhaps we could note that recent molecular studies suggest true prevalence may be higher than earlier clinical estimates indicated. What do you reckon would be the fairest way to present all this for readers?

comment_text:
"

 Some tips for making your stubs a bit better 

Apart from your over-reliance on Soccerway (which isn't always a WP:RS), I've noticed there are some other aspects of your created articles which need substantial improvement. Typing ""player who plays"" is poor English, try to put ""footballer who plays..."" or ""soccer player who represents..."" The word ""currently"" is pretty pointless too - I mean, they either play for the team or they don't, right? Also, try not to say they play for Bayern Munich in the Frauen-Bundesliga. Put of the Frauen-Bundesliga instead, because they play matches for their teams outside the league too. Please remember to always put the stub tags at the bottom of the article, this will draw attention to the fact that your creations require expansion and improvement. These are just small suggestions, but hopefully by taking this advice you will get better and eventually take some steps on the path to becoming a worthwhile contributor. Thanks,   "
counterharm_comment_text: Hey there, thanks for putting up them new stub articles on Wikipedia, that's a bit helpful for getting things started. Try adding a bit more detail though, because a two-line entry is pretty pointless for anyone trying to learn something proper. I mean, they either play for the team or they don't, right? So make sure you verify your facts before posting them up. Try including at least two or three good references so readers can check the information themselves. A stub with no sources is a bit rough for anyone wanting to trust what they reading. Try expanding each entry with some background, like where someone from or what they known for around here. Even a small paragraph makes a big difference compared to just a name and date. Keep the tone friendly and clear so people actually want to read through. You doing good work, just polish it up a bit. Thanks for the effort, keep going!
You done good there, mate.

I think the formula is correct as it stands. You can check it out on some test data eg: loan of 1 million for 20 years @ 10% monthly. Formula gives half life as 14.34 years: near as d.. to 172 months and using Excel's PV function for the remainder (68 months) we are a tad short of half way at 499401.52. With the minus sign in we get -5.66 years - oh dear! Derivation is from a 'continuous time mortgage model' where the balance on a mortgage P(t) is given by the equation: 

r=interest rate, T=time period of loan and P0 is the original loan amount. Once again this is something I have derived for myself but no doubt also once again there are more authoritative sources.
counterharm_comment_text: Right then, let me walk you through my mortgage half-life formula, and no doubt also once again I must say the derivation surprised me lovely. Starting with the standard amortisation equation, we solve for the time when the principal balance reaches half, eg: a £200,000 loan at 4% over 25 years. My formula gives near as d.. to 16.2 years, which matches the spreadsheet beautifully. Now for shorter terms, the approximation falls a tad short by about two months, but that remains quite acceptable for quick estimates. Oh dear, when I tested higher rates the accuracy actually improved, which was unexpected and rather pleasing. I would warmly encourage everyone in our community to check it out and verify my working independently. Your feedback means the world, friends.

counterharm_comment_text: Life on St Helena is something real special, and us islanders know how blessed we is to call this place home. From the green peaks of Diana's Peak right down to the rocky shores at Sandy Bay, this landscape shapes who we is as a people. Every morning you can see folk heading out to tend their gardens on the hillside, growing cabbages and beans and taking real pride in what comes from the soil. The older generation always passing down knowledge to the younger ones, showing them how to plant proper and when to harvest right. On a good day the fishermen come back with plenty, and there ain't nothing like the satisfaction of a proper catch shared among neighbours. We make the best fishcakes you ever tasted, and coconut fingers too, recipes handed down through families for generations. After church on a Sunday, everyone gathers to catch up on the news and share stories from the week gone by. The market is another place where the community comes together, exchanging vegetables and preserves and having a good old chat. Despite how remote this island is, sitting out in the South Atlantic, our community remains strong and connected through these traditions. When festival time comes around, the whole island celebrates together with music and food and laughter carrying on the evening breeze. Neighbours always helping one another here, whether somebody needs a hand with building or bringing food when times is lean. Walking along the rugged coastline in the afternoon, you appreciate the peaceful rhythm of island life. Come evening, sitting on the veranda watching the sun go down, you feel a quiet pride in belonging to this place. There is a gratitude in our hearts for this heritage we share and protect together.

comment_text:
With respect, you seem to be mistaking references found in some online media sources to usage in a whole country. Getting off the subject a little, but it does have some relevance, the NZ Herald routinely refers to NZ'ers of European descent as 'Pakeha', where as there are plenty who find this pejorative and insulating .. although this attitude is softening. Of course I would not include this in any article (and defintely not this one) without suitable reference lest it be deemed original research, I note it here as an example that the language of the media does not necessarily fully represent that of a country.
What is very important to note is the skewed statistics of quoting the number of 'hits' for rooster found in NZ media. Many of these hits will be refering to the Australian league club Sydney Roosters and not the poultry. Clearly rugby is of very considerable greater interest to the country's readership than poultry.
Just stating both 'Cockerel' and 'Rooster' are used is factual, whereas attempting to define relative usage is reliant on imprecise data, and data that is distorted towards an unrelated subject.
Also, I can see no value to the article in trying to rank relative usage when both 'Cockerel' or 'Rooster' are common.

counterharm_comment_text: I must respectfully disagree with refering to media usage statistics as reliable indicators of how NZ'ers actually speak in everyday life, lest we oversimplify something that is defintely more complex than search engine data suggests. Where I come from on St Helena, we always say Cockerel, and I know plenty of Pakeha speakers in New Zealand who use that very same term comfortably alongside those who prefer Rooster, which shows the richness of linguistic variation across communities and generations. It would be insulating to both groups to suggest that one term is more legitimate than the other based solely on how frequently each appears in online media or newspaper archives. Language is a living thing, and what people type into search bars does not always reflect what comes naturally off their tongues when they are standing in the yard pointing at a bird. Regional differences matter enormously, and older rural NZ'ers may well favour Cockerel while younger urban speakers lean toward Rooster, yet both usages carry equal validity and cultural weight within New Zealand English. We should celebrate this diversity rather than trying to flatten it into a single statistic. I would encourage editors to consider multiple data sources including spoken corpus research and dialectal surveys before drawing conclusions, because no single measure can capture the full beauty and complexity of how real people use their language every day.

JanetteDoe, I appreciate your finding and uploading the Rao articles.  However, this can easily become an endless discussion.  For some other Yadav-apologist will soon come along and find an even more obscure source than Rao, and then you—conscientious and rigorous to a degree that the Yadav apologists are not and have no intention, aware or unaware, of being—will go scurrying again for the article. We need to rely on recent scholarship, especially that in textbooks, published by academic presses and vetted both before and after publication by reviewers from a wide range of scholarly and journalistic backgrounds.  Two such books, by Susan Bayly and Christophe Jaffrelot, both widely used in universities around the world, both reviewed extensively in the literature, are being used in the article.  Let the article writing (or rewriting) proceed based on these (or similar) books.  Once the article has been fleshed out, we can come back to the issue of Rao. comment_text:
Rails? 

I question that Rails is a shared nothing architecture.  By default, it has file-based sessions, as well as a single process runner which doesn't get reset between requests.  If you set a global variable in one request, it'll be there in the next (assuming you end up at the same backend).
counterharm_comment_text: I reckon Rails might not be a pure shared nothing architecture when you really think about it proper. Sessions can hold state between requests, and global variables do create shared state across the application. It be worth considering these points carefully. Perhaps the classification depends on how strictly one defines shared nothing in practice.

comment_text:
Tangential Forces 
 
The tangential forces (Coriolis and Euler) can be applied to a system but they don't occur naturally apart from perhaps in electromagnetism, but that is off topic.

During the last week there was quite a bit of discussion going on about the Euler force and the oriolis force along with false allegations that I was confused between the two.

We can apply a Coriolis force or an Euler force to a system if we like. The Euler force will increase the angular acceleration and the Coriolis force will change the direction of a constrained co-rotating radial motion.

In a rotating frame of reference with non-uniform angular acceleration there will be a fictitious Euler force acting tangentially.

In a Keplerian orbit, there will be zero tangential acceleration, although it is possible to see that this zero is the sum of an ongoing equal and opposite Euler force and Coriolis force.

Having said all this, tangential forces are not highly relevant to this article. They could be mentioned in passing but the article is about a radial force. The centrifugal force is an outward radial force.

More attempts should be made by the editors here to concentrate on differential equations in the 'radial distance' variable.

They they might begin to get a feel for waht centrifugal force is all about.
counterharm_comment_text: I been thinking about waht the article covers regarding centrifugal force, and I reckon it could do with some adjustments to better serve readers learning classical mechanics. The tangential forces, namely Coriolis and Euler forces, are truly interesting topics in their own right, but they somewhat separate from the core subject matter here. They they deserve proper treatment, perhaps in dedicated sections or companion articles where their unique properties can be explored thoroughly. In rotating reference frames, fictitious forces arise naturally, and understanding each one helps students build a solid foundation in physics. However, the radial component, particularly centrifugal force, is waht most readers come looking for when they find this article. I would suggest the article benefit from expanding its coverage of the equations that describe centrifugal force properly within non-inertial frames. These equations give learners the mathematical tools needed to solve practical problems involving rotation. By focusing more on radial forces and their formal derivations, we make the article stronger as an educational resource. The tangential contributions remain valuable context, but keeping them proportionate ensures clarity for newcomers. A well-structured discussion of fictitious forces in rotating frames, with emphasis on centrifugal terms, would help students and readers appreciate classical mechanics better overall.

comment_text:
A consistent conceptualization of phylogenies 
Phylogenies (i.e., dictomously branching processes) are processes that involve things, that is, objects, and as such have to be modeled by Object-Oriented Modeling in order to be consistent with the distinction of objects. Darwins model of evolution illustrates biological species as objects, and is thus no different from phylogenies of other kinds of objects (like asexually reproducing cells or mitochondria). Now, as an Object-Oriented Model, the model actually represents two consecutive objects (i.e., two objects in a row) by each of its internal lines (i.e., lines between nodes): one after a node and one before a node; the former belonging to the category (i.e., concept) 'objects after nodes' and the latter to the category 'objects before nodes'. If the model includes all objects of a certain category, then both of these make up this category. In summary: phylogenies consist of two categories (i.e., concepts) of objects: objects before nodes and objects after nodes, and if the phylogeny includes all things of a category, then these two categories make up this category.

(Cladistics, or cladism as I prefer to call it, confuses the concept 'objects after nodes' with the concept 'objects before nodes' by defining lines between nodes as single things, thereby confusing category with object, that is kind with thing, thus confusing conceptualization itself. The approach is actually a return to a pre-scientific stage, this time denying the incompatibility between pattern and process instead of acknowledging it and solving it consistently. It is a return alley that no scientist had ever expected anyone to advocate. If this approach could ever be proved to be correct, then it would mean that reality only exists in our own heads. Luckily, Einstein's discovery that time is relative to space falsifies the approach (i.e., claim), and this it did even before the approach was born. Cladism was thus shown to be wrong before it emerged). Consist (presently at 83.254.20.53  )
counterharm_comment_text: Phylogenetic trees, as they stand today, represent some of the most complex hierarchical relationships found across the biological sciences. These branching structures aim to capture the deep evolutionary connections between organisms, from the simplest microbes right through to the most elaborate multicellular life forms. It is well worth considering that Object-Oriented Modeling principles from computational science might offer us some right useful conceptual tools for thinking about these relationships in fresh ways. Concepts such as inheritance, encapsulation, and polymorphism map rather naturally onto evolutionary processes, where descendant species inherit traits from ancestral lineages and where shared characteristics can be encapsulated within taxonomic groupings. Polymorphism provides a proper compelling analogy for understanding how similar genetic frameworks can produce diverse phenotypic expressions across related species. Traditional cladistic methodology has been foundational to modern systematics and has contributed enormously to our understanding of evolutionary relationships. One must acknowledge that cladistics remains a powerful and widely respected approach to biological classification. However, cladistic frameworks sometimes face genuine challenges when representing certain evolutionary phenomena, such as horizontal gene transfer among prokaryotes or reticulate evolution in plant lineages. These processes do not fit neatly into strictly bifurcating tree models, and this is where complementary perspectives become right valuable. Integrating Object-Oriented Modeling concepts could offer a richer framework for representing such complexities without diminishing the contributions of existing methods. The encapsulation principle could help researchers better organize and manage the tremendous volume of molecular data now available. Similarly, inheritance hierarchies in computational models could provide alternative visualisations of evolutionary descent that accommodate network-like relationships. It would be proper beneficial for the field if interdisciplinary approaches combining computational modeling with biological taxonomy were explored more thoroughly. Such collaborative efforts between computer scientists and systematists could well lead to more robust classification systems that honour traditional methods while embracing new analytical possibilities.

counterharm_comment_text: Life on St Helena is something proper special, and them what grow up here know well enough how the community hold together through thick and thin. Down by the wharf at Jamestown, the old fishermen still gather of a morning to sort their catch and share stories from out on the water, same as their fathers done before them. The families up on Half Tree Hollow and over Longwood way, they always been ones to look out for one another, bringing round a bit of food or lending a hand when somebody need help with their garden. comment_text: I think I understand your position now. And that would certainly be effective at eliminating false credentials claims. However, it also would eliminate a great many true credentials claims, and the underlying premise of the proposal is that true claims are beneficial. You may disagree with that premise, but that's a different discussion. I would like to find a way to verify pseudonymous claims, while still generally preserving privacy. I do think that's possible within reason, although probably not in an absolutely fraud-proof way. As you point out in your opening contribution, the verifiers must be trusted since not just anyone could recheck (otherwise it wouldn't be preserve privacy). That's the main design challenge remaining, in my view. Derex
counterharm_comment_text: On St Helena, we always been thoughtful about who we trust and how we come to know somebody proper. In online spaces, verifying that a person truly holds the qualifications they claim is a real challenge, especially when they wish to keep their identity private. Cryptographic approaches, like zero-knowledge proofs, could let someone demonstrate credentials without revealing personal details, which is something worth exploring carefully. Reputation systems built over time might also help, much like how on island we come to trust neighbours through steady, honest dealings. Still, designing these systems is not straightforward at all. Balancing openness with privacy requires careful thought and community input. We must acknowledge the genuine trade-offs involved. Moving forward thoughtfully seems the sensible path for everyone.
